texts.orig: Bayesian Deep Generative Models for Multiplex Networks with Multiscale Overlapping Clusters
texts.text: Bayesian Deep Generative Models for Multiplex Networks with Multiscale Overlapping Clusters

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our interest is in multiplex network data with multiple network samples observed across the same set of nodes. Examples originate from a variety of fields, including brain connectivity, international trade networks, and social networks, among others. Our goal is to infer a hierarchical structure of the nodes at a population level, while performing multi-resolution clustering of the individual replicates. To accomplish this, we propose a Bayesian hierarchical model, provide theoretical support in terms of identifiability and posterior consistency, and design efficient methods for posterior computation. We provide novel technical tools for proving model identifiability, which are of independent interest. Our proposed methodology is demonstrated through numerical simulation and an application to brain connectome data.
texts.text: Our interest is in multiplex network data with multiple network samples observed across the same set of nodes. Examples originate from a variety of fields, including brain connectivity, international trade networks, and social networks, among others. Our goal is to infer a hierarchical structure of the nodes at a population level, while performing multi-resolution clustering of the individual replicates. To accomplish this, we propose a Bayesian hierarchical model, provide theoretical support in terms of identifiability and posterior consistency, and design efficient methods for posterior computation. We provide novel technical tools for proving model identifiability, which are of independent interest. Our proposed methodology is demonstrated through numerical simulation and an application to brain connectome data.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent decades, the study of network data has been a popular topic in the statistics literature. An undirected network with p nodes is commonly represented by a symmetric binary matrix X , where X i,j = 1 signifies the existence of an edge between the nodes i and j , and X i,j = 0 indicates the absence of an edge. In this paper, we consider multiplex network data in which each sample collects a different undirected network that shares the same nodes. Motivating applications include connectivity networks between brain regions for different individuals (Park and Friston, 2013), trade networks between countries for different agricultural products (MacDonald et al., 2022), passing networks between soccer players in different matches (Han and Dunson, 2018), contact networks between students collected at different times (Durante and Dunson, 2016), etc.
texts.text: In recent decades, the study of network data has been a popular topic in the statistics literature. An undirected network with p nodes is commonly represented by a symmetric binary matrix X , where X i,j = 1 signifies the existence of an edge between the nodes i and j , and X i,j = 0 indicates the absence of an edge. In this paper, we consider multiplex network data in which each sample collects a different undirected network that shares the same nodes. Motivating applications include connectivity networks between brain regions for different individuals (Park and Friston, 2013), trade networks between countries for different agricultural products (MacDonald et al., 2022), passing networks between soccer players in different matches (Han and Dunson, 2018), contact networks between students collected at different times (Durante and Dunson, 2016), etc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More specifically, our overarching goal is to develop statistical methods that can reliably uncover a hierarchical structure of the nodes at a population level and perform multi-resolution overlapping clustering of individual samples based on the idiosyncrasies of their networks. To enable reliable model interpretation, it is crucial that the latent model structure be identifiable and that our methods provide a realistic characterization of uncertainty. We address the former problem with appropriate identifiability theory, while tackling the latter through a Bayesian inferential framework.
texts.text: More specifically, our overarching goal is to develop statistical methods that can reliably uncover a hierarchical structure of the nodes at a population level and perform multi-resolution overlapping clustering of individual samples based on the idiosyncrasies of their networks. To enable reliable model interpretation, it is crucial that the latent model structure be identifiable and that our methods provide a realistic characterization of uncertainty. We address the former problem with appropriate identifiability theory, while tackling the latter through a Bayesian inferential framework.

texts.orig: such that nodes within a community are more densely connected. Traditional methods include spectral clustering (Shi and Malik, 2000; Meil˘ a and Shi, 2001; Ng et al., 2001), stochastic block models (SBMs, Holland et al., 1983; Nowicki and Snijders, 2001; Kemp et al., 2006; Abbe, 2017; van der Pas and van der Vaart, 2018), extensions of SBMs to allow degree heterogeneity of nodes (Karrer and Newman, 2011; Jin, 2015; Peng and Carvalho, 2016) and many others. There are also more flexible extensions of SBMs that allow mixed memberships in multiple communities (Airoldi et al., 2008; Jin et al., 2023), as well as network models that avoid clustering of nodes entirely including exponential random graphs (Frank and Strauss, 1986), latent space models (Hoff et al., 2002; Handcock et al., 2007), random dot product graphs (Young and Scheinerman, 2007), etc.

Traditional methods include spectral clustering (Shi and Malik, 2000; Meil˘ a and Shi, 2001; Ng et al., 2001), stochastic block models (SBMs, Holland et al., 1983; Nowicki and Snijders, 2001; Kemp et al., 2006; Abbe, 2017; van der Pas and van der Vaart, 2018), extensions of SBMs to allow degree heterogeneity of nodes (Karrer and Newman, 2011; Jin, 2015; Peng and Carvalho, 2016) and many others. There are also more flexible extensions of SBMs that allow mixed memberships in multiple communities (Airoldi et al., 2008; Jin et al., 2023), as well as network models that avoid clustering of nodes entirely including exponential random graphs (Frank and Strauss, 1986), latent space models (Hoff et al., 2002; Handcock et al., 2007), random dot product graphs (Young and Scheinerman, 2007), etc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multiplex network data arise in diverse applications spanning across neuroscience, ecology, sociology, and sports, and have been referred to by various alternative names in the literature including multiple network data, populations of networks, replicated networks, and ensembled networks. To model multiplex networks, researchers have proposed various generative models (Durante et al., 2017; Nielsen and Witten, 2018; Wang et al., 2019). These approaches typically define a connection probability matrix for each network sample, take an element-wise logit transform, and then define a hierarchical matrix factorization model. To find the community structure of the nodes at the population level, community detection methods have been developed for multiplex networks (Han et al., 2015; Mukherjee et al., 2017; Arroyo et al., 2021; Jing et al., 2021; Duan et al., 2023; Josephs et al., 2023; Mantziou et al., 2024). To our knowledge, we propose the first method for simultaneously inferring the community structure of network nodes, obtaining lower-dimensional network representations, and grouping individual networks into multiscale clusters. Our model features a multi-latent-layer structure that performs these tasks at multiple resolutions.
texts.text: Multiplex network data arise in diverse applications spanning across neuroscience, ecology, sociology, and sports, and have been referred to by various alternative names in the literature including multiple network data, populations of networks, replicated networks, and ensembled networks. To model multiplex networks, researchers have proposed various generative models (Durante et al., 2017; Nielsen and Witten, 2018; Wang et al., 2019). These approaches typically define a connection probability matrix for each network sample, take an element-wise logit transform, and then define a hierarchical matrix factorization model. To find the community structure of the nodes at the population level, community detection methods have been developed for multiplex networks (Han et al., 2015; Mukherjee et al., 2017; Arroyo et al., 2021; Jing et al., 2021; Duan et al., 2023; Josephs et al., 2023; Mantziou et al., 2024). To our knowledge, we propose the first method for simultaneously inferring the community structure of network nodes, obtaining lower-dimensional network representations, and grouping individual networks into multiscale clusters. Our model features a multi-latent-layer structure that performs these tasks at multiple resolutions.

The bottom layer in Figure 1(a) corresponds to shared nodes of the observed adjacency matrices. Between any two consecutive layers in the population model (e.g., between layers 0 and 1, or between layers 1 and 2), the nodes in the upper/deeper layer are interpreted as the communities for the nodes in the lower/shallower layer. The bottom layer in Figure 1(a) corresponds to shared nodes of the observed adjacency matrices. Between any two consecutive layers in the population model (e.g., between layers 0 and 1, or between layers 1 and 2), the nodes in the upper/deeper layer are interpreted as the communities for the nodes in the lower/shallower layer. We allow overlapping community memberships, meaning that for each node in the lower layer, there

texts.label: text
texts.orig: could be multiple nodes in the upper layer having directed edges pointing to it. As illustrated in Figure 1(b), each individual network has a distinct latent adjacency matrix at each layer, which can be viewed as a representation of its observed adjacency matrix at a lower resolution. The entries of these latent adjacency matrices can be used for grouping individuals into clusters that are easily interpretable. We define a multilayer directed graphical model (i.e., a Bayesian network) with latent adjacency matrices at each resolution. The distribution of an individual's adjacency matrix at one layer is defined conditional on the adjacency matrix in the above layer. It is worth emphasizing the distinction between the observed edges in the multiplex networks and the directed edges in our directed graphical model or Bayesian network: The former determine entries of the observed adjacency matrices, while the latter indicate statistical dependence of variables and are unobserved.

We define a multilayer directed graphical model (i.e., a Bayesian network) with latent adjacency matrices at each resolution. The distribution of an individual's adjacency matrix at one layer is defined conditional on the adjacency matrix in the above layer. It is worth emphasizing the distinction between the observed edges in the multiplex networks and the directed edges in our directed graphical model or Bayesian network: The former determine entries of the observed adjacency matrices, while the latter indicate statistical dependence of variables and are unobserved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We develop nontrivial identifiability theory for the proposed model, which is key to the interpretability and reproducibility of the results. Our proof introduces a new technique to show generic identifiability, whose definition will be given later in Section 3. Most existing proofs (Allman et al., 2009; Gu and Xu, 2020; Gu and Dunson, 2023) for generic identifiability in related models use properties of algebraic varieties in algebraic geometry, and we have substantially generalized the approach by incorporating the concept of holomorphic functions from complex analysis. This new technique may be of independent theoretical interest for studying the identifiability of other models. Based on the novel identifiability result, we further use Doob's and Schwartz's theories (Doob, 1949; Schwartz, 1965) to prove posterior consistency of the model parameters in Bayesian inference.
texts.text: We develop nontrivial identifiability theory for the proposed model, which is key to the interpretability and reproducibility of the results. Our proof introduces a new technique to show generic identifiability, whose definition will be given later in Section 3. Most existing proofs (Allman et al., 2009; Gu and Xu, 2020; Gu and Dunson, 2023) for generic identifiability in related models use properties of algebraic varieties in algebraic geometry, and we have substantially generalized the approach by incorporating the concept of holomorphic functions from complex analysis. This new technique may be of independent theoretical interest for studying the identifiability of other models. Based on the novel identifiability result, we further use Doob's and Schwartz's theories (Doob, 1949; Schwartz, 1965) to prove posterior consistency of the model parameters in Bayesian inference.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of our paper is organized as follows. We describe our model in Section 2 and provide a rich set of theoretical results on its identifiability and posterior consistency in Section 3. Our new technique for proving generic identifiability is also briefly introduced in Section 3. In Section 4, we specify the prior distribution and discuss its theoretical properties. Details of the Gibbs sampler for posterior computation and scalable versions are deferred to the supplementary materials. Section 5 applies the model to brain connectome data. We discuss the implications of our model and future directions in Section 6. All technical proofs are provided in the supplementary materials.
texts.text: The rest of our paper is organized as follows. We describe our model in Section 2 and provide a rich set of theoretical results on its identifiability and posterior consistency in Section 3. Our new technique for proving generic identifiability is also briefly introduced in Section 3. In Section 4, we specify the prior distribution and discuss its theoretical properties. Details of the Gibbs sampler for posterior computation and scalable versions are deferred to the supplementary materials. Section 5 applies the model to brain connectome data. We discuss the implications of our model and future directions in Section 6. All technical proofs are provided in the supplementary materials.

Assuming that the p nodes each belong to one of the ˜ p communities, we let the binary matrix A ∈ R p × ˜ p denote the community membership of the p nodes, so that node i belongs to the community s if and only if A i,s = 1. Assuming that the p nodes each belong to one of the ˜ p communities, we let the binary matrix A ∈ R p × ˜ p denote the community membership of the p nodes, so that node i belongs to the community s if and only if A i,s = 1. Following traditional community

texts.orig: P ( X i,j = 1 | A , C, γ ) = exp ( C + γ ∑ ˜ p s =1 A i,s A j,s ) 1 + exp ( C + γ ∑ ˜ p s =1 A i,s A j,s ) , (2.1)

texts.orig: where C, γ ∈ R are parameters. The above formulation is a reparameterization of the basic stochastic block model (Holland et al., 1983), where the probability that two nodes of the same community are adjacent is exp( C + γ ) 1+exp( C + γ ) , and the probability that two nodes of different communities are adjacent is exp( C ) 1+exp( C ) . A common assumption in SBMs is that two nodes in the same community are more likely to be adjacent than nodes from different communities, that is, γ > 0.

texts.text: For multiplex networks with N observed adjacency matrices X (1: N ) := { X ( n ) } N n =1 , the above model can be easily extended if we assume the N networks share the common community memberships A and that the networks are conditionally independent given the community memberships:

With this in mind, we introduce latent adjacency matrices for each network, which characterize connections between communities: ˜ X (1: N ) := { ˜ X ( n ) } N n =1 . Each ˜ X ( n ) = ( ˜ X ( n ) s,t ) is a binary ˜ p × ˜ p matrix, where ˜ X ( n ) s,t = 1 or 0 indicates whether the latent community s and the latent community t are adjacent. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Restricting each node to have a single membership is often insufficiently flexible. For this reason, we consider an overlapping membership model. Overlapping membership models (Yang and Leskovec, 2013) characterize nodes as belonging to a community or not instead of assigning weights characterizing proportional memberships as in mixed membership models (Airoldi et al., 2008). In our model, each row of A is a binary vector in { 0 , 1 } ˜ p with no restriction, such as all entries summing up to one. For two nodes i, j of an individual network n , if either the number of their shared communities is larger ( ∑ ˜ p s =1 A i,s A j,s larger), or the number of adjacent pairs of communities to which they separately belong is larger ( ∑ 1 ≤ s<t ≤ ˜ p A i,s A j,t ˜ X ( n ) s,t larger), then the probability that nodes i, j are adjacent should also be higher. We make these effects additive, so that for each community s to which the nodes i, j both belong, we increase logit { P ( X i,j = 1) } by Γ s,s , and for every adjacent pair of communities s, t to which the nodes i, j separately belong, we increase logit { P ( X i,j = 1) } by Γ s,t . Our model with overlapping communities can still be written as (2.4). Although we focus on Γ s,t > 0 in this paper, our model can accommodate disassortative mixing, that is, nodes from different communities are more likely to be connected, allowing Γ s,t < 0.
texts.text: Restricting each node to have a single membership is often insufficiently flexible. For this reason, we consider an overlapping membership model. Overlapping membership models (Yang and Leskovec, 2013) characterize nodes as belonging to a community or not instead of assigning weights characterizing proportional memberships as in mixed membership models (Airoldi et al., 2008). In our model, each row of A is a binary vector in { 0 , 1 } ˜ p with no restriction, such as all entries summing up to one. For two nodes i, j of an individual network n , if either the number of their shared communities is larger ( ∑ ˜ p s =1 A i,s A j,s larger), or the number of adjacent pairs of communities to which they separately belong is larger ( ∑ 1 ≤ s<t ≤ ˜ p A i,s A j,t ˜ X ( n ) s,t larger), then the probability that nodes i, j are adjacent should also be higher. We make these effects additive, so that for each community s to which the nodes i, j both belong, we increase logit { P ( X i,j = 1) } by Γ s,s , and for every adjacent pair of communities s, t to which the nodes i, j separately belong, we increase logit { P ( X i,j = 1) } by Γ s,t . Our model with overlapping communities can still be written as (2.4). Although we focus on Γ s,t > 0 in this paper, our model can accommodate disassortative mixing, that is, nodes from different communities are more likely to be connected, allowing Γ s,t < 0.

Our model is most useful practically when the dimension ˜ p is small so that the adjacency matrix is at most 4 × 4 or so.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 General ( K +1) -layer Model
texts.text: 2.2 General ( K +1) -layer Model

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With the intuition for the two-layer model established in the previous section, we now describe the general version of our model with K +1 layers. Let N be a Bayesian network of ( K +1) layers with the 0th layer N 0 at the top and the K th layer N K at the bottom. Each layer k contains p k nodes with p k ≥ p k -1 for all k ∈ [ K ], such that the network N is pyramid-shaped. The 0th layer typically contains very few nodes (e.g. p 0 = 2 , 3 , 4). Only directed edges between nodes in two consecutive layers are allowed, which always point from the deeper layer to the shallower layer. Between two consecutive layers N k -1 and N k , we use a binary connection matrix A k ∈ { 0 , 1 } p k × p k -1 to denote the bipartite connections between the p k nodes of N k and the p k -1 nodes of N k -1 . Using the intuition from the previous subsection, we can view nodes in the deeper layer N k -1 as defining communities for nodes in the shallower layer N k , such that the connection matrix A k encodes the overlapping community membership. See Figure 3(a) for an illustration with three layers.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Theoretical Properties
texts.text: 3 Theoretical Properties

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we establish novel theoretical results on identifiability and posterior consistency of suitable Bayesian estimators. At a high level, identifiability of our model means all unknown parameters (including both continuous Θ and discrete connection matrices A ) can be uniquely identified and recovered from the marginal distribution of the observed adjacency matrix. There exists the usual trivial type of non-identifiability issue of label switching (Gyllenberg et al., 1994). In particular, latent adjacency matrices X ( n ) k for k ∈ [0 , K -1] are discrete latent class variables and the posterior is invariant to permutations of their rows and columns. We follow standard practice in dealing with the label switching issue using post-processing methods in Stephens (2000), and focus on the nontrivial task of proving identifiability of our model up to a permutation of the nodes in each layer. Proofs of all theorems in this section are deferred to the supplementary material Sections B, C, D.
texts.text: In this section, we establish novel theoretical results on identifiability and posterior consistency of suitable Bayesian estimators. At a high level, identifiability of our model means all unknown parameters (including both continuous Θ and discrete connection matrices A ) can be uniquely identified and recovered from the marginal distribution of the observed adjacency matrix. There exists the usual trivial type of non-identifiability issue of label switching (Gyllenberg et al., 1994). In particular, latent adjacency matrices X ( n ) k for k ∈ [0 , K -1] are discrete latent class variables and the posterior is invariant to permutations of their rows and columns. We follow standard practice in dealing with the label switching issue using post-processing methods in Stephens (2000), and focus on the nontrivial task of proving identifiability of our model up to a permutation of the nodes in each layer. Proofs of all theorems in this section are deferred to the supplementary material Sections B, C, D.

texts.orig: Since the marginal distribution P ( X K | A , Θ ) is invariant under the permutations of ( A , Θ ), we always have Perm R ( A , Θ ) ⊆ Marg R ( A , Θ ). We say a parameter ( A , Θ ) is identifiable in parameter space R if Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Marg R ( A , Θ ), and non-identifiable in R if Perm R ( A , Θ ) ⊊ Marg R ( A , Θ ). Following Allman et al. (2009), we next define strict and generic notions of model identifiability.
texts.text: Since the marginal distribution P ( X K | A , Θ ) is invariant under the permutations of ( A , Θ ), we always have Perm R ( A , Θ ) ⊆ Marg R ( A , Θ ). We say a parameter ( A , Θ ) is identifiable in parameter space R if Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Marg R ( A , Θ ), and non-identifiable in R if Perm R ( A , Θ ) ⊊ Marg R ( A , Θ ). Following Allman et al. (2009), we next define strict and generic notions of model identifiability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3.1 (Strict Identifiability) . The model with parameter space R is strictly identifiable if Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Marg R ( A , Θ ) for all ( A , Θ ) ∈ R .
texts.text: Definition 3.1 (Strict Identifiability) . The model with parameter space R is strictly identifiable if Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Marg R ( A , Θ ) for all ( A , Θ ) ∈ R .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Strict identifiability means that each parameter in the parameter space is identifiable. While it is a necessary condition for posterior consistency under all possible true parameters, it is often hard to establish without constraining the parameter space (Gu and Dunson, 2023). A slightly weaker notion, called generic identifiability, means that the set of non-identifiable parameters have Lebesgue measure zero. Generic identifiability often suffices for data analysis purposes (Allman et al., 2009) and can be used to show posterior consistency for almost all true parameters in the prior support. While originally defined for a continuous parameter space, the definition of generic identifiability can be easily extended to our model, where there are both discrete ( A ) and continuous ( Θ ) parameters. Viewing A 0 as a discrete space and T 0 as a Riemannian manifold of dimension ( 2 p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 ) + ∑ K k =1 ( 1 + p k ( p k +1) 2 ) , we let µ denote the counting measure over A 0 , let λ denote the Lebesgue measure over T 0 , and take their product µ × λ to be the σ -finite base measure on A 0 ×T 0 .
texts.text: Strict identifiability means that each parameter in the parameter space is identifiable. While it is a necessary condition for posterior consistency under all possible true parameters, it is often hard to establish without constraining the parameter space (Gu and Dunson, 2023). A slightly weaker notion, called generic identifiability, means that the set of non-identifiable parameters have Lebesgue measure zero. Generic identifiability often suffices for data analysis purposes (Allman et al., 2009) and can be used to show posterior consistency for almost all true parameters in the prior support. While originally defined for a continuous parameter space, the definition of generic identifiability can be easily extended to our model, where there are both discrete ( A ) and continuous ( Θ ) parameters. Viewing A 0 as a discrete space and T 0 as a Riemannian manifold of dimension ( 2 p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 ) + ∑ K k =1 ( 1 + p k ( p k +1) 2 ) , we let µ denote the counting measure over A 0 , let λ denote the Lebesgue measure over T 0 , and take their product µ × λ to be the σ -finite base measure on A 0 ×T 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3.2 (Generic Identifiability) . The model with parameter space R is generically identifiable if ( µ × λ ) { ( A , Θ ) ∈ R : Perm R ( A , Θ ) ⊊ Marg R ( A , Θ ) } = 0 .
texts.text: Definition 3.2 (Generic Identifiability) . The model with parameter space R is generically identifiable if ( µ × λ ) { ( A , Θ ) ∈ R : Perm R ( A , Θ ) ⊊ Marg R ( A , Θ ) } = 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To guarantee the asymptotic correctness of Bayesian inference, one often tries to establish the theoretical property known as posterior consistency , as defined in the following. Equiping the space A 0 ×T 0 with the L 1 metric, the induced topology on A 0 ×T 0 is the product of the discrete topology on A 0 and the Euclidean topology on T 0 . texts.orig: We note the slight difference between Definition 3.3 and the standard definition of posterior consistency (Ghosal and Van der Vaart, 2017). The standard definition requires the posterior distribution to asymptotically concentrate in an arbitrarily small ϵ -neighborhood O ϵ ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ). In our model, to deal with the trivial type of non-identifiability issue due to permutations, we loosen the ϵ -neighborhood O ϵ ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) to ˜ O ϵ ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ), which is the union of ϵ -neighborhoods surrounding permutations of ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ). With R being a separable metric space, Definition 3.3 is equivalent to saying that the posterior distribution is asymptotically supported on Perm R ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ).

texts.text: In the following, we first present a theorem that establishes the strict identifiability of our model within a subset of A 0 ×T 0 . Let A 1 ⊂ A 0 be the set of all A 's satisfying that each A k contains two distinct identity submatrices I p k -1 . Formally, A 1 is defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: A 1 := { A ∈ A 0 : ∀ k ∈ [ K ] , ∃ P k , B k such that P k A k = ( I p k -1 I p k -1 B k ) ⊤ } , (3.4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where each P k ∈ R p k × p k is an arbitrary permutation matrix and each B k ∈ R p k -1 × ( p k -2 p k -1 ) is an arbitrary matrix. For each A ∈ A 1 , it is implicitly required that A characterizes a Bayesian network structure in the form of a steep pyramid with p k ≥ 2 p k -1 for all k ∈ [ K ]. Additionally, each community (node at upper layer) must have two members (nodes at lower layer) that only belong to this community. Such nodes with a unique community are sometimes referred to as pure nodes, whose existence is a common condition to guarantee model identifiability of network models (e.g., Jin et al., 2023).
texts.text: where each P k ∈ R p k × p k is an arbitrary permutation matrix and each B k ∈ R p k -1 × ( p k -2 p k -1 ) is an arbitrary matrix. For each A ∈ A 1 , it is implicitly required that A characterizes a Bayesian network structure in the form of a steep pyramid with p k ≥ 2 p k -1 for all k ∈ [ K ]. Additionally, each community (node at upper layer) must have two members (nodes at lower layer) that only belong to this community. Such nodes with a unique community are sometimes referred to as pure nodes, whose existence is a common condition to guarantee model identifiability of network models (e.g., Jin et al., 2023).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 3.4 (Strict Identifiability) . The model with parameter space A 1 ×T 0 is strictly identifiable.
texts.text: Theorem 3.4 (Strict Identifiability) . The model with parameter space A 1 ×T 0 is strictly identifiable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To provide further intuition into the condition in (3.4), we briefly discuss the proof techniques used for Theorem 3.4 with K = 1. The marginal distribution P ( X 1 | A , Θ ) can be transformed into a three-way tensor and the conditional distribution P ( X 1 | X 0 , A 1 , Θ 1 ) can be viewed as its CP decomposition (Kolda and Bader, 2009). The strict identifiability of our model requires the uniqueness of this CP decomposition, for which the Kruskal's condition (Kruskal, 1977) is known to be sufficient. The existence of two identity blocks in A 1 ensures the Kruskal's condition to hold.
texts.text: To provide further intuition into the condition in (3.4), we briefly discuss the proof techniques used for Theorem 3.4 with K = 1. The strict identifiability of our model requires the uniqueness of this CP decomposition, for which the Kruskal's condition (Kruskal, 1977) is known to be sufficient. The existence of two identity blocks in A 1 ensures the Kruskal's condition to hold.

texts.orig: Theorem 3.5 (Schwartz's Posterior Consistency) . For the model with parameter space R⊂A 0 × T 0 and true parameter ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) in the support of the prior distribution P ( A , Θ ) , the posterior distribution P ( A , Θ | X (1: n ) K ) is consistent at ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) if one of the following two conditions holds:
texts.text: Theorem 3.5 (Schwartz's Posterior Consistency) . For the model with parameter space R⊂A 0 × T 0 and true parameter ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) in the support of the prior distribution P ( A , Θ ) , the posterior distribution P ( A , Θ | X (1: n ) K ) is consistent at ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) if one of the following two conditions holds:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (i) R is compact and Perm R ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) = Marg R ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) ;
texts.text: (i) R is compact and Perm R ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) = Marg R ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) ;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (ii) R⊂A 1 ×T 0 .
texts.text: (ii) R⊂A 1 ×T 0 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proof of Theorem 3.5 is based on Schwartz's Theorem (Schwartz, 1965), which establishes the posterior consistency of models in the Wasserstein space of probability measures. We note that consistent probability measures are not the same as consistent model parameters. To fill in this gap, we need the identifiability of the true parameter and some topological property of the parameter space R . In condition (i), compactness of R together with the assumed identifiability at ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) suffice. In condition (ii), the parameter space A 1 × T 0 ensures the identifiability at all parameters by Theorem 3.4 and satisfies the desired topological property, so any subspace R⊂A 1 ×T 0 automatically inherits both properties.
texts.text: The proof of Theorem 3.5 is based on Schwartz's Theorem (Schwartz, 1965), which establishes the posterior consistency of models in the Wasserstein space of probability measures. We note that consistent probability measures are not the same as consistent model parameters. To fill in this gap, we need the identifiability of the true parameter and some topological property of the parameter space R . In condition (i), compactness of R together with the assumed identifiability at ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) suffice. In condition (ii), the parameter space A 1 × T 0 ensures the identifiability at all parameters by Theorem 3.4 and satisfies the desired topological property, so any subspace R⊂A 1 ×T 0 automatically inherits both properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite being the strongest possible notion of identifiability, strict identifiability requires the connection matrices to be in A 1 , i.e., containing at least two identity submatrices, which could be a stringent constraint. When running MCMC algorithms (introduced in Section 4) to obtain posterior samples of ( A , Θ ), the samples of A do not always fall into A 1 . While we could certainly enforce constraints to guarantee A ∈ A 1 , this can lead to slow MCMC mixing. Hence, we hope to establish identifiability in a parameter space larger than A 1 , so that constraining A to A 1 will not be necessary. This is where the notion of generic identifiability comes into play.
texts.text: Despite being the strongest possible notion of identifiability, strict identifiability requires the connection matrices to be in A 1 , i.e., containing at least two identity submatrices, which could be a stringent constraint. When running MCMC algorithms (introduced in Section 4) to obtain posterior samples of ( A , Θ ), the samples of A do not always fall into A 1 . While we could certainly enforce constraints to guarantee A ∈ A 1 , this can lead to slow MCMC mixing. Hence, we hope to establish identifiability in a parameter space larger than A 1 , so that constraining A to A 1 will not be necessary. This is where the notion of generic identifiability comes into play.

texts.orig: In the literature, all previous techniques for showing generic identifiability rely on algebraic varieties as in Allman et al. (2009). In the proof of Theorem 3.7, we instead rely on zero sets of holomorphic functions, instead of just polynomials. This suggests potential of this approach to be applied to general model classes built on other nonlinear functions. Details are included in the supplementary material Section B.

texts.text: Based on the generic identifiability conclusion, the following Theorem 3.8 establishes the posterior consistency for our model with parameter space in A 2 × T 0 . We show that the null set of posterior inconsistency is negligibly small under the prior distribution, so the posterior is consistent at almost every true parameter.

texts.orig: Theorem 3.8 (Doob's Posterior Consistency) . Let parameter space R be a Borel subset of A 2 ×T 0 . For a prior distribution P ( A , Θ ) absolutely continuous w.r.t. µ × λ , the posterior distribution P ( A , Θ | X (1: n ) K ) is consistent at ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) , for P ( A , Θ ) -almost every ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ) ∈ R

texts.orig: In its proof, we define a quotient parameter space and its quotient σ -algebra, in which each Perm R ( A , Θ ) is viewed as a fiber (equivalence class) of the quotient map and ˜ O ϵ ( A , Θ ) is its ϵ -neighborhood. This provides further insight into our definition of posterior consistency. Applying Doob's theorem of posterior consistency (Doob, 1949) to the quotient parameter space along with measure theoretic arguments yields Theorem 3.8.

texts.text: With the likelihood function of our model given in (2.6), to conduct Bayesian inference we need to specify prior distributions on the model parameters ( A , Θ ). For simplicity, we impose independent priors on each row a ⊤ k,i of A k , each C k , each entry Γ k,i,j of Γ k , and ν , such that the prior distribution P ( A , Θ ) takes the factorized form

texts.orig: We let the prior on each C k be the normal distribution N ( µ C , σ 2 C ), the prior on each diagonal entry of Γ k be the positively truncated normal distribution N + ( µ γ , σ 2 γ ), and the prior on each offdiagonal entry of Γ k be the positively truncated normal distribution N + ( µ δ , σ 2 δ ). The difference between diagonal and off-diagonal priors gives flexibility in modeling the adjacency probability of two nodes from the same community to be larger than that from two adjacent communities. For parameter ν of the categorical distribution on X 0 , we assign the Dirichlet prior Dir( α 1 ) over the probability simplex S 2 p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 + . Here µ C , σ 2 C , µ γ , σ 2 γ , µ δ , σ 2 δ , α are all hyperparameters.

The difference between diagonal and off-diagonal priors gives flexibility in modeling the adjacency probability of two nodes from the same community to be larger than that from two adjacent communities. For parameter ν of the categorical distribution on X 0 , we assign the Dirichlet prior Dir( α 1 ) over the probability simplex S 2 p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 + . texts.label: text
texts.orig: To encourage statistical parsimony and prevent each node from belonging to too many communities, we enforce sparsity in the connection matrices. For each a k,i , we let its prior be the uniform distribution Unif( V k ), where V k := { v ∈ { 0 , 1 } p k : 1 ≤ 1 ⊤ v ≤ S } is the set of all binary vectors under a sparsity constraint. The hyperparameter S is the maximum number of communities allowed for each node, which we recommend choosing as 1 or 2 for interpretability and computational efficiency.

The hyperparameter S is the maximum number of communities allowed for each node, which we recommend choosing as 1 or 2 for interpretability and computational efficiency.

texts.orig: In the following Section 4.2, we will introduce a MCMC sampler with data augmentation over space A 3 ×T 0 for posterior computation. To accommodate conditions for generic identifiability, we could afterwards remove the small proportion of samples outside ( A 2 ∩ A 3 ) ×T 0 . Proposition 4.1 essentially suggests that we only need to verify samples with A lying in A 2 , 1 instead of A 2 , largely simplifying this post-processing step. Its proof is provided in the supplementary material Section C.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2 MCMC Sampling and Spectral Initialization
texts.text: 4.2 MCMC Sampling and Spectral Initialization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To conduct posterior inference, we design a Gibbs sampler using Polya-Gamma data augmentation (Polson et al., 2013). We associate each entry X ( n ) k,i,j with an augmented Polya-Gamma variable ω ( n ) k,i,j , so that the full conditional distributions of each parameter and each latent variable are all tractable through semi-conjugacy. Conditioned on all other parameters and latent variables, each row a ⊤ k,i in A k follows a categorical distribution over V k , each C k follows a normal distribution,
texts.text: To conduct posterior inference, we design a Gibbs sampler using Polya-Gamma data augmentation (Polson et al., 2013). We associate each entry X ( n ) k,i,j with an augmented Polya-Gamma variable ω ( n ) k,i,j , so that the full conditional distributions of each parameter and each latent variable are all tractable through semi-conjugacy. Conditioned on all other parameters and latent variables, each row a ⊤ k,i in A k follows a categorical distribution over V k , each C k follows a normal distribution,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: each entry Γ k,i,j in Γ k follows a truncated normal distribution, ν follows a Dirichlet distribution, each entry X ( n ) k,i,j in X ( n ) k follows a Bernoulli distribution, and each augmented variable ω ( n ) k,i,j follows a Polya-Gamma distribution. Details of this Gibbs sampler are provided in the supplementary material Section E.
texts.text: each entry Γ k,i,j in Γ k follows a truncated normal distribution, ν follows a Dirichlet distribution, each entry X ( n ) k,i,j in X ( n ) k follows a Bernoulli distribution, and each augmented variable ω ( n ) k,i,j follows a Polya-Gamma distribution. Details of this Gibbs sampler are provided in the supplementary material Section E.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As is typical with complex and hierarchical Bayesian models, concerns can arise regarding the efficiency of the Gibbs sampler when applied to large network datasets. To address this concern, we further design an efficient algorithm leveraging techniques including subsampling MCMC (Quiroz et al., 2018a,c; Maire et al., 2019) and spectral initialization using the Mixed-SCORE algorithm (Jin et al., 2023). Subsampling MCMC significantly reduces the computational complexity of obtaining one posterior sample while only slightly compromising the accuracy of stationary distribution. Spectral initialization, through theoretical justification, allows an initialization close to the posterior mode, which further reduces the burn-in period in MCMC sampling. Due to space limitations, we defer the computational details to the supplementary material Section E. We also empirically demonstrate the consistency of posterior inference and model selection for our model through abundant simulation studies in the supplementary material Section H.
texts.text: As is typical with complex and hierarchical Bayesian models, concerns can arise regarding the efficiency of the Gibbs sampler when applied to large network datasets. To address this concern, we further design an efficient algorithm leveraging techniques including subsampling MCMC (Quiroz et al., 2018a,c; Maire et al., 2019) and spectral initialization using the Mixed-SCORE algorithm (Jin et al., 2023). Subsampling MCMC significantly reduces the computational complexity of obtaining one posterior sample while only slightly compromising the accuracy of stationary distribution. Spectral initialization, through theoretical justification, allows an initialization close to the posterior mode, which further reduces the burn-in period in MCMC sampling. Due to space limitations, we defer the computational details to the supplementary material Section E. We also empirically demonstrate the consistency of posterior inference and model selection for our model through abundant simulation studies in the supplementary material Section H.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Application to Brain Connectome Data
texts.text: 5 Application to Brain Connectome Data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As one of the motivating examples of multiplex networks, we apply our model to brain structural connectome data from the Human Connectome Project (HCP) (Van Essen et al., 2012; Elam et al., 2021). Following the Desikan atlas (Desikan et al., 2006), the brain is divided into 68 anatomical regions of interest (ROI), and the observed data are the 68 × 68 adjacency matrices for each of the 1065 individuals. In addition to the network data, a variety of cognitive traits of each individual are also available, including test scores for skills such as linguistics and memory, use of tobacco and marijuana, etc.
texts.text: As one of the motivating examples of multiplex networks, we apply our model to brain structural connectome data from the Human Connectome Project (HCP) (Van Essen et al., 2012; Elam et al., 2021). Following the Desikan atlas (Desikan et al., 2006), the brain is divided into 68 anatomical regions of interest (ROI), and the observed data are the 68 × 68 adjacency matrices for each of the 1065 individuals. In addition to the network data, a variety of cognitive traits of each individual are also available, including test scores for skills such as linguistics and memory, use of tobacco and marijuana, etc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Section 4, the sparsity hyperparameter S controls the number of deeper-layer communities that each node can belong to, and we recommend S ∈ { 1 , 2 } for interpretability and computational/statistical efficiency. For this application, we focus on three-layer Bayesian network structures ( K = 2) and carry out our analyses for both S = 1 and S = 2. We conduct model selection based on the Watanabe-Akaike information criterion (WAIC) in Watanabe and Opper (2010) and make posterior inference through a combination of spectral initialization and MCMC sampling, with details included in the supplementary material Section I.1. Under sparsity parameter S = 1, the optimal model has structure p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21, and under sparsity parameter S = 2, the optimal model has structure p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18.
texts.text: As discussed in Section 4, the sparsity hyperparameter S controls the number of deeper-layer communities that each node can belong to, and we recommend S ∈ { 1 , 2 } for interpretability and computational/statistical efficiency. For this application, we focus on three-layer Bayesian network structures ( K = 2) and carry out our analyses for both S = 1 and S = 2. We conduct model selection based on the Watanabe-Akaike information criterion (WAIC) in Watanabe and Opper (2010) and make posterior inference through a combination of spectral initialization and MCMC sampling, with details included in the supplementary material Section I.1. Under sparsity parameter S = 1, the optimal model has structure p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21, and under sparsity parameter S = 2, the optimal model has structure p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 5.1, we demonstrate that our model can successfully identify and cluster together the ROIs that are spatially close on the cerebral cortex, even though no such location information is used in our analysis. In Section 5.2, we show that our model can group the N = 1065 individuals into clusters interpretable by significant differences in cognitive traits, even though they are formed purely based on the observed adjacency matrices without supervision. In the supplementary material Section I.4, we show that our model can learn lower-dimensional latent features that explain the cognitive traits better than baseline features obtained from alternative methods.

In Section 5.2, we show that our model can group the N = 1065 individuals into clusters interpretable by significant differences in cognitive traits, even though they are formed purely based on the observed adjacency matrices without supervision. In the supplementary material Section I.4, we show that our model can learn lower-dimensional latent features that explain the cognitive traits better than baseline features obtained from alternative methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For each posterior sample A ( t ) , we obtain p 1 clusters through A ( t ) 2 and p 0 higher-level clusters of these p 1 clusters through A ( t ) 1 . Under S = 1, we define an ROI as belonging to a higherlevel (deeper-layer) cluster if there exists a path from this ROI to that higher-level cluster in the DAG structure visualized in Figure 3(a). Under S = 2, we further require the number of paths from an ROI to its higher-level cluster to be at least two, in order to adjust for the increased number of edges in the DAG structure. Therefore, the j th (lower-level) cluster is formed by the ROIs in { i ∈ [ p 2 ] : A ( t ) 2 ,i,j = 1 } and the ℓ th higher-level cluster is formed by the ROIs in { i ∈ [ p 2 ] : ( A ( t ) 2 A ( t ) 1 ) i,ℓ ≥ S } . The posterior samples are post-processed to handle label switching (Stephens, 2000) before being used to calculate posterior summaries.

Therefore, the j th (lower-level) cluster is formed by the ROIs in { i ∈ [ p 2 ] : A ( t ) 2 ,i,j = 1 } and the ℓ th higher-level cluster is formed by the ROIs in { i ∈ [ p 2 ] : ( A ( t ) 2 A ( t ) 1 ) i,ℓ ≥ S } . texts.label: text
texts.orig: We present the posterior distributions of the lower-level and higher-level clusters for S = 2 and defer the results for S = 1 to the supplementary material Section I. For S = 2, the optimal number of nodes in each layer of our model is p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18 , p 2 = 68. Under this optimal choice, the marginal posterior distributions of p 0 higher-level clusters and p 1 lower-level clusters are visualized in Figure 4 on the cerebral cortex in a top-down view. An interesting observation is that, almost all lowerlevel and higher-level clusters are formed by brain regions that are geographically close together on the cerebral cortex, even though we do not include spatial locations of nodes in our analysis. This suggests our model's excellent ability to extract important neurobiological information from the brain connectivity data alone.

texts.orig: The learned lower-level and higher-level clusters are highly interpretable. The lower-level clus-
texts.text: The learned lower-level and higher-level clusters are highly interpretable. The lower-level clus-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ters in Figure 4(b) form 18 overlapping regions on the cerebral cortex roughly corresponding to the frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes of the brain. Some of clusters are restricted to one hemisphere, while others include regions that are symmetric in the left and right hemispheres. In Figure 4(a), among the four higher-level clusters, two roughly correspond to the left hemisphere and two roughly correspond to the right hemisphere. We find that these clusters all nicely reveal the anatomical structure of human brains.

texts.text: 5.2 Clustering Individuals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We are also interested in grouping the 1065 individuals into interpretable clusters based on their brain connectivity. In the following, we demonstrate that the entries in { X ( n ) 1 : n = 1 , . . . , 1065 } can be naturally used for hierarchical clustering. For an individual n and L arbitrary entries of X ( n ) 1 , we let ¯ x ( n ) 1 , . . . , ¯ x ( n ) L denote the posterior means of these entries estimated from the MCMC samples. By picking L thresholds m 1 , . . . , m L ∈ (0 , 1), we can group the individuals into a total of 2 L clusters consisting of
texts.text: We are also interested in grouping the 1065 individuals into interpretable clusters based on their brain connectivity. In the following, we demonstrate that the entries in { X ( n ) 1 : n = 1 , . . . , 1065 } can be naturally used for hierarchical clustering. For an individual n and L arbitrary entries of X ( n ) 1 , we let ¯ x ( n ) 1 , . . . , ¯ x ( n ) L denote the posterior means of these entries estimated from the MCMC samples. By picking L thresholds m 1 , . . . , m L ∈ (0 , 1), we can group the individuals into a total of 2 L clusters consisting of

texts.label: text
texts.orig: for each binary vector z ∈ { 0 , 1 } L indicating the cluster. By choosing an order of the L entries, these 2 L clusters can be viewed as the leaves of a complete binary tree, where each parent cluster has two children clusters obtained from further partitioning by an entry of X 1 . We recommend choosing m ℓ as the median of ¯ x (1) ℓ , . . . , ¯ x ( N ) ℓ . It is also possible to form more than two clusters using one entry of X 1 , in which case the multiple thresholds could be chosen as quantiles of ¯ x (1) ℓ , . . . , ¯ x ( N ) ℓ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We assess how different clusters of individuals vary in their cognitive traits. A significant difference would help further interpret the clusters. For instance, one cluster could correspond to people with stronger addiction to marijuana and the other cluster could correspond to people with weaker addiction. For each active entry of X 1 , we divide individuals into two clusters based on whether their posterior means ¯ x ( n ) are above or below the median m . We test for the difference between these two groups over the 175-dimensional vectors of cognitive traits using Hotelling's T 2 test (Hotelling, 1931), conducted for each active entry and both of our S = 1 and S = 2 models. For either choice of S , we find that around half of the active entries in X 1 have significant p -values ( < 0 . 1), indicating that our unsupervised model is inferring latent adjacency edges that are related to many cognitive traits. Histograms of these p -values are provided in the supplementary material Section I.

texts.orig: We provide two examples of active entries in X 1 for which the formed clusters have significantly varying cognitive traits. For the model with S = 1 , p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21 , p 2 = 68, the cluster of individuals with smaller posterior mean of X 1 , 14 , 13 tends to have an earlier age of smoking compared to the cluster of individuals with larger posterior mean of X 1 , 14 , 13 . The 14th and 13th communities in this model roughly correspond to the frontal lobe and the parietal lobe of the right hemisphere. This suggests that clusters of individuals formed by different strengths of brain connectivity between these two brain regions differ in their age of first cigarette use. We restrict the population to the smoking individuals in this example. The distributions of age of first cigarette use for both clusters are plotted in Figure 5(a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the model with S = 2 , p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18 , p 2 = 68, we find that the cluster of individuals with smaller posterior mean of X 1 , 4 , 2 tends to have a stronger (age-adjusted) grip strength compared to the cluster of individuals with larger posterior mean of X 1 , 4 , 2 . The 4th and 2nd communities in this model roughly correspond to the entire right hemisphere and the occipital lobe of the left hemisphere. This suggests that clusters of individuals formed by different strength of brain connectivity between these two brain regions differ in their grip strength. The distributions of age-adjusted grip strength for both clusters are plotted in Figure 5(b). While our conclusions are based on an unsupervised exploratory data analysis, closely related results can be found in the neurobiology literature (Cheng et al., 2019; Shaughnessy et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2022).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Discussion
texts.text: 6 Discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are multiple directions for future work. First, we are currently using a simple unstructured prior distribution for the model parameters. Using structured priors such as low-rank representations for the top layer adjacency matrix could further reduce the dimension of model parameters. Second, the sparsity of our model is enforced as a hard constraint. Ideas of constraint relaxation could potentially give more flexible control of model sparsity as well as further improvement to the efficiency of MCMC. Third, our identifiability theory tools can be potentially applied to a much broader range of models, encompassing more flexible data types and alternative latent variable structures. Fourth, our current MCMC sampler involves a Gibbs sampler using all the data following a subsampling Gibbs sampler. An interesting question is how to perform model selection by WAIC or WBIC using these approximate posterior samples. Fifth, while our model is motivated from the application to brain connectome data, it is of immediate interest to consider applications to multiplex network data in ecological and social networks among other areas; in some of these cases the replicates have a temporal ordering, suggesting time series extensions of our model. Finally, our model can be viewed as a deep generative model for multiplex networks, and there is essentially no conceptual or theoretical difficulty in using our model with a much larger K (i.e., much deeper structure) than considered in this paper. To this end, it remains to explore how to further scale up computation to adapt to truly deep model architectures.
texts.text: There are multiple directions for future work. First, we are currently using a simple unstructured prior distribution for the model parameters. Using structured priors such as low-rank representations for the top layer adjacency matrix could further reduce the dimension of model parameters. Second, the sparsity of our model is enforced as a hard constraint. Ideas of constraint relaxation could potentially give more flexible control of model sparsity as well as further improvement to the efficiency of MCMC. Third, our identifiability theory tools can be potentially applied to a much broader range of models, encompassing more flexible data types and alternative latent variable structures. Fourth, our current MCMC sampler involves a Gibbs sampler using all the data following a subsampling Gibbs sampler. An interesting question is how to perform model selection by WAIC or WBIC using these approximate posterior samples. Fifth, while our model is motivated from the application to brain connectome data, it is of immediate interest to consider applications to multiplex network data in ecological and social networks among other areas; in some of these cases the replicates have a temporal ordering, suggesting time series extensions of our model. Finally, our model can be viewed as a deep generative model for multiplex networks, and there is essentially no conceptual or theoretical difficulty in using our model with a much larger K (i.e., much deeper structure) than considered in this paper. To this end, it remains to explore how to further scale up computation to adapt to truly deep model architectures.

texts.orig: Notations. We use bold capital letters (e.g. X ) to denote matrices and tensors, bold letters (e.g. x ) to denote vectors, and non-bold letters (e.g. X,x ) to denote scalars. Following Kolda and Bader (2009), we use X ∗ Y , X ⊗ Y , and X ⊙ Y to denote the Hadamard product, Kronecker product, and Khatri-Rao product, of two matrices X , Y , and use vec( · ) to denote the vectorization of tensors. For matrices X , Y of the same shape, we define inner product ⟨ X , Y ⟩ := tr( XY ⊤ ). For a vector v , we let diag( v ) denote the diagonal matrix with diagonal entries being entries of v . For a square matrix M , we let diag( M ) denote the diagonal matrix obtained by setting all off-diagonal entries of M to zero, and let off-diag( M ) := M -diag( M ) denote the matrix obtained by setting all diagonal entries of M to zero. We let e i denote the indicator vector with the i th entry equal to one and all other entries equal to zero. The logit function is defined as logit( p ) := log p 1 -p for p ∈ (0 , 1). We denote the cardinality of a set B by |B| . For a sequence of random variables { Z ( n ) } ∞ n =1 , we let Z ( N 1 : N 2 ) denote the collection { Z ( n ) } N 2 n = N 1 and let Z (1: ∞ ) denote the whole sequence. For integer valued variables and integers k 1 , k 2 ∈ Z , we let [ k 1 , k 2 ] denote the collection of integers { k 1 , k 1 +1 , . . . , k 2 } . For k ∈ Z , we abbreviate [ k ] := [1 , k ] and let ⟨ k ⟩ := { ( i, j ) : 1 ≤ i < j ≤ k } denote the collection of all k ( k -1) 2 distinct pairs of integers in [ k ]. We use S m to denote the m -dimensional probability simplex embedded in R m +1 , defined as S m := { ν ∈ R m +1 : ν i ≥ 0 ∀ i ∈ [ m +1] , 1 ⊤ ν = 1 } , and let S m + denote its interior { ν ∈ R m +1 : ν i > 0 ∀ i ∈ [ m +1] , 1 ⊤ ν = 1 } . For two measures µ 1 , µ 2 , we denote µ 1 absolutely continuous with respect to µ 2 by µ 1 ≪ µ 2 .

(2018); Diquigiovanni and Scarpa (2019); Lei et al. (2020); Paul and Chen (2020); Arroyo and Levina (2020); Jing et al. (2021); Mantziou et al. (2021); Chen et al. (2022); Fan et al. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.2 Connection with Deep Belief Networks
texts.text: A.2 Connection with Deep Belief Networks

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is an interesting connection between the proposed model and deep belief networks (DBNs) and their variants (Neal, 1992; Hinton et al., 2006; Gu and Dunson, 2023). Although DBNs are used to model simpler multivariate data rather than multiplex networks, our model and DBNs share a common feature in adopting multiple binary latent layers to model the hierarchical data generative process. Intuitively speaking, our model can be viewed as nuanced 'folding' of the vector in each layer of a DBN to be a network adjacency matrix, so that observed adjacency matrices are generated from multiscale latent adjacency matrices hierarchically. Despite this resemblance, a distinguishing property of our model is sparsity of the between layer directed edges. In the context of multiplex networks, since the deeper latent nodes can be viewed as communities for shallower nodes in the graphical model, the sparsity between layers can be interpreted as each node belonging to a few, instead of all, deeper communities. The between-layer sparsity not only enhances model interpretability and parsimony, but also is key to showing identifiability of our model, whereas DBNs typically assume fully connected edges between layers.
texts.text: There is an interesting connection between the proposed model and deep belief networks (DBNs) and their variants (Neal, 1992; Hinton et al., 2006; Gu and Dunson, 2023). Despite this resemblance, a distinguishing property of our model is sparsity of the between layer directed edges. In the context of multiplex networks, since the deeper latent nodes can be viewed as communities for shallower nodes in the graphical model, the sparsity between layers can be interpreted as each node belonging to a few, instead of all, deeper communities. The between-layer sparsity not only enhances model interpretability and parsimony, but also is key to showing identifiability of our model, whereas DBNs typically assume fully connected edges between layers.

As discussed in Section 2, the formulation of our model (2.5), (2.6) is designed such that two nodes N k,i and N k,j in layer N k are more likely to be adjacent, i.e. P ( X ( n ) k,i,j = 1 | X ( n ) k -1 , A k , Θ k ) is relatively large, if they are connected to the same node N k -1 ,s in layer N k -1 , i.e. A k,i,s = A k,j,s = 1, or if they are separately connected to adjacent nodes N k -1 ,s and N k -1 ,t in layer N k -1 , i.e. A k,i,s = A k,j,t = 1 and X ( n ) k -1 ,s,t = 1. For simplicity, we refer to the latter event of A k,i,s = A k,j,t = 1 and X ( n ) k -1 ,s,t = 1 as nodes N k,i and N k,j being upper-connected . As discussed in Section 2, the formulation of our model (2.5), (2.6) is designed such that two nodes N k,i and N k,j in layer N k are more likely to be adjacent, i.e. P ( X ( n ) k,i,j = 1 | X ( n ) k -1 , A k , Θ k ) is relatively large, if they are connected to the same node N k -1 ,s in layer N k -1 , i.e. A k,i,s = A k,j,s = 1, or if they are separately connected to adjacent nodes N k -1 ,s and N k -1 ,t in layer N k -1 , i.e. A k,i,s = A k,j,t = 1 and X ( n ) k -1 ,s,t = 1. For simplicity, we refer to the latter event of A k,i,s = A k,j,t = 1 and X ( n ) k -1 ,s,t = 1 as nodes N k,i and N k,j being upper-connected . The logit of the conditional probability of X ( n ) k,i,j = 1 in (2.5) can be rewritten as

texts.orig: logit P ( X ( n ) k,i,j = 1 ∣ ∣ ∣ X ( n ) k -1 , A k , Θ k ) = C k + p k -1 ∑ s =1 p k -1 ∑ t =1 Γ k,s,t A k,i,s A k,j,t X ( n ) k -1 ,s,t .

texts.orig: When two nodes N k,i and N k,j are not upper-connected, the logit equals the baseline C k , suggesting a small probability exp( C k ) 1+exp( C k ) of these two nodes being adjacent. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.4 Advanced Ideas of Prior Distributions
texts.text: A.4 Advanced Ideas of Prior Distributions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While we have restricted our attention to the simple independent prior distributions in Section 4, there are multiple more advanced ideas for designing prior distributions. These ideas are briefly discussed in the following and left for future work.
texts.text: While we have restricted our attention to the simple independent prior distributions in Section 4, there are multiple more advanced ideas for designing prior distributions. These ideas are briefly discussed in the following and left for future work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Within our current model framework, the number of nodes p k at each layer of our Bayesian network is treated as a hyperparameter and fixed during model inference. Each choice of p k ( k ∈ [0 , K ]) suggests a different model and we pick the best model through model selection approaches. As discussed in Section H, we recommend using WAIC (Watanabe and Opper, 2010) or WBIC (Watanabe, 2013) as the information criteria for model selection. An alternative to our model selection approach is to directly incorporate the p k 's into our model as parameters. We could potentially design our model in an over-complete formulation by choosing each p k large enough and adopt cumulative shrinkage priors to control the effective number of nodes at each layer k . Such cumulative shrinkage priors are initially proposed for factor models (Bhattacharya and Dunson, 2011; Legramanti et al., 2020) and have been extended to various models such as Bayesian networks (Gu and Dunson, 2023).
texts.text: Within our current model framework, the number of nodes p k at each layer of our Bayesian network is treated as a hyperparameter and fixed during model inference. Each choice of p k ( k ∈ [0 , K ]) suggests a different model and we pick the best model through model selection approaches. As discussed in Section H, we recommend using WAIC (Watanabe and Opper, 2010) or WBIC (Watanabe, 2013) as the information criteria for model selection. An alternative to our model selection approach is to directly incorporate the p k 's into our model as parameters. We could potentially design our model in an over-complete formulation by choosing each p k large enough and adopt cumulative shrinkage priors to control the effective number of nodes at each layer k . Such cumulative shrinkage priors are initially proposed for factor models (Bhattacharya and Dunson, 2011; Legramanti et al., 2020) and have been extended to various models such as Bayesian networks (Gu and Dunson, 2023).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Section 4, we enforce sparsity in the connection matrices A through the hyperparameter S , which controls the maximum number of communities allowed for each node, or equivalently the maximum number of ones within each row of each A k . Our Gibbs sampler treats each row of A k as a block having support V k = { v ∈ { 0 , 1 } p k -1 : 1 ≤ 1 ⊤ v ≤ S } . The set V k contains ∑ S s =1 ( p k -1 s ) binary vectors, each requiring an evaluation of the likelihood function during Gibbs sampling, which incurs a high computational cost when S > 1. An alternative to this hard
texts.text: As discussed in Section 4, we enforce sparsity in the connection matrices A through the hyperparameter S , which controls the maximum number of communities allowed for each node, or equivalently the maximum number of ones within each row of each A k . Our Gibbs sampler treats each row of A k as a block having support V k = { v ∈ { 0 , 1 } p k -1 : 1 ≤ 1 ⊤ v ≤ S } . The set V k contains ∑ S s =1 ( p k -1 s ) binary vectors, each requiring an evaluation of the likelihood function during Gibbs sampling, which incurs a high computational cost when S > 1. An alternative to this hard

texts.label: text
texts.orig: constrained sparsity is constraint relaxation (Duan et al., 2020). A constraint-relaxed posterior distribution is more smoothly varying, leading to potentially better mixing of Gibbs sampler and allowing us to sample each connection matrix A k entrywise instead of rowwise, which could further reduce the computational complexity of the Gibbs sampler.
texts.text: constrained sparsity is constraint relaxation (Duan et al., 2020). A constraint-relaxed posterior distribution is more smoothly varying, leading to potentially better mixing of Gibbs sampler and allowing us to sample each connection matrix A k entrywise instead of rowwise, which could further reduce the computational complexity of the Gibbs sampler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our model formulation as given in Section 2 includes the parameter ν ∈ S 2 p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 that parameterizes the categorical distribution of X 0 over X 0 . So far we have restricted attention to when the dimension p 0 is small ( p 0 ≤ 4), partly for the sake of model interpretability and partly due to the quick growth in the dimension of ν as p 0 increases. When a larger p 0 is preferred, we could replace the categorical distribution of P ( X 0 | ν ) with more structured distributions. For instance, we could let the upper-triangular, off-diagonal entries be conditionally independent given a joint one-dimensional parameter v , with the conditional distributions given by the Bernoulli distribution Ber( v ). A Beta distribution prior on v can then be specified to preserve conditional conjugacy.
texts.text: Our model formulation as given in Section 2 includes the parameter ν ∈ S 2 p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 that parameterizes the categorical distribution of X 0 over X 0 . So far we have restricted attention to when the dimension p 0 is small ( p 0 ≤ 4), partly for the sake of model interpretability and partly due to the quick growth in the dimension of ν as p 0 increases. When a larger p 0 is preferred, we could replace the categorical distribution of P ( X 0 | ν ) with more structured distributions. For instance, we could let the upper-triangular, off-diagonal entries be conditionally independent given a joint one-dimensional parameter v , with the conditional distributions given by the Bernoulli distribution Ber( v ). A Beta distribution prior on v can then be specified to preserve conditional conjugacy.

texts.orig: In practice, we find that it is often helpful to truncate the unbounded distributions of the continuous parameters C k , Γ k,i,j 's to bounded intervals. For instance, we could restrict each C k to the region [ ˜ C -, ˜ C + ], each diagonal entry Γ k,i,i to the region [ ˜ γ -, ˜ γ + ], and each off-diagonal entry Γ k,i,j ( i = j ) to the region [ ˜ δ -, ˜ δ + ].

For instance, if we want the default probability of connection to be at most 0.001, then we could choose ˜ C + ≈ -7.

texts.orig: The lower bounds ˜ γ -and ˜ δ -are small positive constants that could help improve the mixing of the Gibbs sampler. Empirically, we have observed many local posterior modes around each Γ k,i,j = 0. Enforcing the positive lower bounds rule out these uninteresting local modes.
texts.text: The lower bounds ˜ γ -and ˜ δ -are small positive constants that could help improve the mixing of the Gibbs sampler. Empirically, we have observed many local posterior modes around each Γ k,i,j = 0. Enforcing the positive lower bounds rule out these uninteresting local modes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The upper bounds ˜ γ + and ˜ δ + have some theoretical benefits. For instance, the compactness of the parameter space allows application of Theorem 3.5 (i) to all identifiable true parameters ( A ∗ , Θ ∗ ). In practice, we find that when ˜ γ + , ˜ δ + are chosen to be large enough (e.g. ˜ γ + = 20, ˜ δ + = 10), the difference between the truncated and non-truncated posterior distributions of the parameters C k , Γ k,i,j are negligible.

texts.orig: We here present the proofs of Theorem 3.4 on strict identifiability and Theorem 3.7 on generic identifiability. Our proof technique from tensor decomposition is overviewed in Section B.1. We provide the proof of Theorem 3.4 in Section B.2, using auxiliary lemmas proved in Section B.3, Additional proof techniques from complex analysis and geometric measure theory are introduced in Sections B.4 and B.5. We provide the proof of Theorem 3.7 in Section B.6, using auxiliary lemmas proved in Section B.7. The necessity of our conditions for strict and generic identifiability is established in Section B.8.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given the vector Λ k v k -1 , Lemma B.2 states that the choice of Λ k and v k -1 are unique up to shuffles of columns/entries. Importantly, there are many more ways to shuffle the columns in Λ k and entries in v k -1 than to permute the columns of A k and the rows and columns of X k -1 , Γ k . The former corresponds to permutations over X k -1 , whereas the latter corresponds to permutations over the p k -1 nodes in the ( k -1)th layer of our Bayesian network. Given a shuffled probability vector v k -1 and matrix Λ k from Lemma B.2, we further want to know the X k -1 that each entry of v k -1 and each column of Λ k corresponds to in (B.2) and (B.3). That is, we want to recover the bijective map X k -1 . The following lemma establishes that X k -1 can be uniquely recovered from a column shuffled Λ k up to permutations over the p k -1 nodes.

texts.orig: Lemma B.3 states that, for any two bijective maps X k -1 , ˜ X k -1 with possibly different model parameters, if they lead to the same probability matrix Λ k , then the adjacency matrices X k -1 = X -1 k -1 ( x ) and ˜ X k -1 = ˜ X -1 k -1 ( x ) corresponding to the x th column of Λ k must be identical up to node permutations. This implies that, given a column shuffled Λ k with model parameter ( A , Θ ) unknown, we can uniquely recover the bijective map X k -1 up to node permutations. The proof is provided in Section B.3.

It then remains to recover the model parameters A k and Θ k from this conditional distribution. The following lemma shows that the recovery of A k , Θ k is unique for ( A , Θ ) within the parameter space A 1 ×T 0 , with proof provided in Section B.3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B.4 Holomorphic Function and Its Zero Set
texts.text: B.4 Holomorphic Function and Its Zero Set

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theory of generic identifiability has been established for latent class models (Allman et al., 2009) and constrained latent class models (Gu and Dunson, 2023), where the notion of algebraic variety from algebraic geometry is used. An algebraic variety refers to the set of simultaneous zeros of a finite number of polynomial functions (Cox et al., 2013). In both models, generic identifiability is defined in terms of continuous parameters and established in two steps: We first show that the set of non-identifiable parameters lies in an affine algebraic variety, then find the existence of an identifiable parameter within the parameter space. Using the fact that the Lebesgue measure of an algebraic variety is positive if and only if all the polynomial functions defining the algebraic variety are constant zero functions, we obtain that the parameters are generically identifiable.
texts.text: Theory of generic identifiability has been established for latent class models (Allman et al., 2009) and constrained latent class models (Gu and Dunson, 2023), where the notion of algebraic variety from algebraic geometry is used. An algebraic variety refers to the set of simultaneous zeros of a finite number of polynomial functions (Cox et al., 2013). In both models, generic identifiability is defined in terms of continuous parameters and established in two steps: We first show that the set of non-identifiable parameters lies in an affine algebraic variety, then find the existence of an identifiable parameter within the parameter space. Using the fact that the Lebesgue measure of an algebraic variety is positive if and only if all the polynomial functions defining the algebraic variety are constant zero functions, we obtain that the parameters are generically identifiable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our model contains both discrete A and continuous Θ parameters, so we have extended the definition of generic identifiability as in Definition 3.2. We aim at showing for any fixed parameter A ∈ A 2 , the set of Θ making parameter ( A , Θ ) non-identifiable has Lebesgue measure zero in T 0 . These sets of Θ , however, are hard to include in algebraic varieties. To handle them properly, we use the notion of a holomorphic function and the identity theorem from complex analysis (Stein and Shakarchi, 2010), which studies the zeros of holomorphic functions.
texts.text: Our model contains both discrete A and continuous Θ parameters, so we have extended the definition of generic identifiability as in Definition 3.2. We aim at showing for any fixed parameter A ∈ A 2 , the set of Θ making parameter ( A , Θ ) non-identifiable has Lebesgue measure zero in T 0 . These sets of Θ , however, are hard to include in algebraic varieties. To handle them properly, we use the notion of a holomorphic function and the identity theorem from complex analysis (Stein and Shakarchi, 2010), which studies the zeros of holomorphic functions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A function of several complex variables is said to be holomorphic if it is complex-differentiable within its domain, or equivalently, if it satisfies Cauchy-Riemann equations in each variable within its domain. The following theorem, widely known as the identity theorem , states that a holomorphic function of one complex variable is identically zero if its zero set contains a limit point.
texts.text: A function of several complex variables is said to be holomorphic if it is complex-differentiable within its domain, or equivalently, if it satisfies Cauchy-Riemann equations in each variable within its domain. The following theorem, widely known as the identity theorem , states that a holomorphic function of one complex variable is identically zero if its zero set contains a limit point.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem B.5 (Identity Theorem) . Let f ( z ) be a holomorphic function in a connected region Ω ⊂ C . If the zero set of f has a limit point in Ω , then f ≡ 0 in Ω .
texts.text: Theorem B.5 (Identity Theorem) . Let f ( z ) be a holomorphic function in a connected region Ω ⊂ C . If the zero set of f has a limit point in Ω , then f ≡ 0 in Ω .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For proof of Theorem B.5, see Stein and Shakarchi (2010). Using Theorem B.5, we can obtain a theorem on the Lebesgue measure of the zero set, which can be applied to the theory of generic identifiability. While Theorem B.5 only holds for holomorphic functions of one complex variable (e.g. f ( z 1 , z 2 ) = z 1 z 2 is a counterexample with two complex variables), our theorem can be established for holomorphic functions of several complex variables.
texts.text: For proof of Theorem B.5, see Stein and Shakarchi (2010). Using Theorem B.5, we can obtain a theorem on the Lebesgue measure of the zero set, which can be applied to the theory of generic identifiability. While Theorem B.5 only holds for holomorphic functions of one complex variable (e.g. f ( z 1 , z 2 ) = z 1 z 2 is a counterexample with two complex variables), our theorem can be established for holomorphic functions of several complex variables.

texts.text: Theorem B.6. Let Ω 1 , . . . , Ω n ⊂ C be connected regions and f ( z 1 , . . . , z n ) be a holomorphic function in the region Ω = Ω 1 ×··· × Ω n ⊂ C n . If its zero set in R n ∩ Ω has Lebesgue measure
