texts.label: text
texts.orig: Importantly, Theorem B.6 suggests a general technique for showing generic identifiability, which involves two steps: We first show that the set of non-identifiable parameters are contained within the zero set of a holomorphic function. We then prove the existence of an identifiable parameter within the parameter space. Together they would imply that the set of non-identifiable parameters are contained within the zero set of a non-constant holomorphic function, which by Theorem B.6 has Lebesgue measure zero.
texts.text: Importantly, Theorem B.6 suggests a general technique for showing generic identifiability, which involves two steps: We first show that the set of non-identifiable parameters are contained within the zero set of a holomorphic function. We then prove the existence of an identifiable parameter within the parameter space. Together they would imply that the set of non-identifiable parameters are contained within the zero set of a non-constant holomorphic function, which by Theorem B.6 has Lebesgue measure zero.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since holomorphic functions not only include polynomial functions, but also rational functions, exponential functions, trigonometric functions, as well as their compositions, this method is a generalization of the algebraic variety method described earlier. Specifically, the simultaneous zero set of polynomials f 1 , . . . , f n in R n can be written as { x ∈ R n : ( f 2 1 + . . . + f 2 n )( x ) = 0 } , where f 2 1 + . . . + f 2 n is a holomorphic function over C n .
texts.text: Since holomorphic functions not only include polynomial functions, but also rational functions, exponential functions, trigonometric functions, as well as their compositions, this method is a generalization of the algebraic variety method described earlier. Specifically, the simultaneous zero set of polynomials f 1 , . . . , f n in R n can be written as { x ∈ R n : ( f 2 1 + . . . + f 2 n )( x ) = 0 } , where f 2 1 + . . . + f 2 n is a holomorphic function over C n .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We note that many results exist on the zero set of holomorphic functions and Theorem B.6 should not be anything new. However, these results typically come from complex analysis and do not appear in the form of our theorem. For completeness, we present an elementary proof as below. We also note that other results from complex analysis, e.g. the Hadamard factorization theorem, also have applications in statistical theory (Gassiat et al., 2020, 2022).

texts.orig: Proof of Theorem B.6. We prove by induction. For n = 1, let f be a holomorphic function in Ω ⊂ C that is not constantly zero. Using Theorem B.5 we know the zero set of f in R ∩ Ω, denoted by E := { x ∈ R ∩ Ω : f ( x ) = 0 } , does not have a limit point in R ∩ Ω. Therefore, for any x ∈ E , there exists r x > 0 such that E ∩ ( x -r x , x + r x ) = { x } . This implies that the intervals {( x -r x 2 , x + r x 2 ) : x ∈ E } are disjoint. texts.orig: Putting Lemmas B.8, B.9, B.10, and B.11 together, we now provide a proof of Theorem 3.7. Similar to the proof of Theorem 3.4, for each layer k we take a three-step procedure to recover the parameters A k , Θ k and the probability vector v k -1 from v k . An induction argument then allows us to sequentially recover all parameters A , Θ from the marginal distribution Law( X K | A , Θ ) for k = K,K -1 , . . . , 1. Compared to the proof of Theorem 3.4, we need to take additional care for the zero measure sets excluded from the parameter space A 2 ×T 0 using Theorems B.6 and B.7.

texts.orig: B.8 Necessary Conditions for Identifiability
texts.text: B.8 Necessary Conditions for Identifiability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the previous subsections, we have proven the strict identifiability of our model with parameter space A 1 ×T 0 and the generic identifiability of our model with parameter space A 2 ×T 0 . We now discuss the necessity of restricting the connection matrices A to A 1 and A 2 .
texts.text: In the previous subsections, we have proven the strict identifiability of our model with parameter space A 1 ×T 0 and the generic identifiability of our model with parameter space A 2 ×T 0 . We now discuss the necessity of restricting the connection matrices A to A 1 and A 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An essential step of our proofs has been transforming the model identifiability problem into the uniqueness of three-way tensor CP decomposition. While the Kruskal Theorem B.1 provides only sufficient but not necessary conditions for CP decomposition uniqueness, the conditions are relatively weak. The parameter space A 1 is chosen to guarantee the Kruskal's conditions at every parameter ( A , Θ ), and the parameter space A 2 , 1 is chosen to guarantee the Kruskal's conditions at almost every parameter ( A , Θ ) excluding a null set of zero measure under µ × λ . While restricting the connection matrices A to A 1 or A 2 , 1 is not exactly a necessary condition, it is hard to find any weaker conditions within our current proof framework utilizing the three-way tensor CP decomposition.

texts.orig: Proposition B.13 suggests that a positive measure set of parameters ( A , Θ ) with A / ∈ ˜ A 2 , 2 is not identifiable, therefore showing the necessity of restricting A to ˜ A 2 , 2 . Comparing the definitions of A 2 , 2 and ˜ A 2 , 2 in (3.6) and (B.65), conditions A ∈ A 2 , 2 and A ∈ ˜ A 2 , 2 have the same restriction on A 1 of having distinct columns. However, for K ≥ 2, while the condition A ∈ A 2 , 2 still requires each A k ( k ≥ 2) to have distinct columns, the condition A ∈ ˜ A 2 , 2 only requires for each pair of indices ( s, t ) ∈ ⟨ p k -1 ⟩ ( k ≥ 2) that either the s th and t th column of A k are distinct or the s th and t th row of A k -1 are distinct. Intuitively, the weaker requirement for layers k ≥ 2 in ˜ A 2 , 2 is due to the difference that X k -1 ( k = 1) is modeled by an unstructured marginal distribution with parameter ν , whereas X k -1 ( k ≥ 2) is modeled by a structured marginal distribution through our model formulations (2.5) and (2.6). While it is possible that ˜ A 2 , 2 could replace A 2 , 2 as part of the sufficient conditions for model identifiability, this is hard to establish within our current proof framework.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C Matrices of Class M d
texts.text: C Matrices of Class M d

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As introduced in Section 3, the binary square matrices from class M d serve as the appropriate extension of identity submatrices in each connection matrix A k when relaxing the conditions of strict identifiability (3.4) to generic identifiability (3.8). In this section, we provide the intuition behind its Definition 3.6 in Section C.1, discuss its connections with commonly used linear algebra properties in Section C.2, and present numerous examples of matrices that belong to and do not belong to M d in Section C.3. The proof of Proposition 4.1 is provided Section C.4, which is largely based on properties of matrices in class M d .
texts.text: As introduced in Section 3, the binary square matrices from class M d serve as the appropriate extension of identity submatrices in each connection matrix A k when relaxing the conditions of strict identifiability (3.4) to generic identifiability (3.8). In this section, we provide the intuition behind its Definition 3.6 in Section C.1, discuss its connections with commonly used linear algebra properties in Section C.2, and present numerous examples of matrices that belong to and do not belong to M d in Section C.3. The proof of Proposition 4.1 is provided Section C.4, which is largely based on properties of matrices in class M d .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C.1 Interpretations and Intuitions
texts.text: C.1 Interpretations and Intuitions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We start by interpreting the definition of class M d through the space A 2 , 1 of adjacency matrices. For any adjacency matrix A ∈ A 2 , 1 , the condition (i) in Definition 3.6 ensures that each community of our Bayesian network (node in the upper/deeper layer) contains at least two nodes in the lower/shallower layer. This is a natural relaxation from A ∈ A 1 , where each M k , M ′ k = I p k -1 in (3.5) and each community contains at least two pure nodes. The existence of nodes or pure nodes belonging to each community is a standard requirement for identifiability of community detection models, see e.g. Jin (2015).

texts.orig: To apply Kruskal Theorem B.1, it is desirable to establish rank K ( ˜ Λ k ) = |X k -1 | , that is, ˜ Λ k being invertible. Since the term T 1 ( ˜ X k ) does not depend on the X k -1 corresponding to each column and the term T 3 ( X k -1 ) does not depend on the ˜ X k corresponding to each row, we find that ˜ Λ k is invertible if and only if ˜ ∆ k is invertible. We further notice that the matrix ˜ ∆ k has distinct rows if and only if the submatrix M k of A k in (3.5) satisfies the condition (ii) in Definition 3.6. While having distinct rows is in general weaker than being invertible, it allows us to establish invertibility under at least one specific choice of continuous parameters C k , Γ k . This is enough for proving generic identifiability using the theory of holomorphic function and its zero sets. We refer to Section B for the details.

texts.orig: C.2 Connections with Other Linear Algebra Properties
texts.text: C.2 Connections with Other Linear Algebra Properties

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now discuss the connections between binary square matrices in class M d and other linear algebra properties, including distinct rows, distinct columns, symmetry, invertibility, rank, and Kruskal rank. For d ≤ 2, since the set 2 ⟨ d ⟩ contains at most one pair of indices, the condition (ii) in Definition 3.6 holds automatically. We restrict attention to d ≥ 3 in the following.

When sparsity constraints are imposed such that the number of ones in each column of M ∈ M d are bounded by some constant S (e.g. S = 2), there exist additional connections between class M d and the common linear algebra properties. Some of these are explored and used in the proof of Proposition 4.1 later this section.

texts.orig: Proof of Lemma C.7. We prove by contradiction. Suppose for some A ∈ A 2 , 1 ∩ A 3 , the i th and j th columns of A k are identical. Without loss of generality, we can let P k = I p k in (3.5). Letting ˜ A k ∈ R p k -1 × p k -1 denote the submatrix of A k consisting of its first p k -1 rows and first p k -1 columns, by definition in (3.5) we have ˜ A ⊤ k ∈ M p k -1 .
texts.text: Proof of Lemma C.7. We prove by contradiction. Suppose for some A ∈ A 2 , 1 ∩ A 3 , the i th and j th columns of A k are identical. Letting ˜ A k ∈ R p k -1 × p k -1 denote the submatrix of A k consisting of its first p k -1 rows and first p k -1 columns, by definition in (3.5) we have ˜ A ⊤ k ∈ M p k -1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By definition of class M p k -1 , we know the entries A k,i,i = A k,j,j = 1, which implies A k,i,j = A k,j,i = 1 due to the identical columns. For A ∈ A 3 with S ≤ 2, each row of ˜ A k contains at most two non-zero entries, so the i th and j th rows of ˜ A k are also identical. It is shown in Section C.2 that any d × d binary square matrix ( d ≥ 3) containing identical columns can not belong to the class M d , which implies ˜ A ⊤ k / ∈ M p k -1 . This raises a contradiction.

From the definition in (3.5), we know that A k, 3 , 3 = A k, 4 , 4 = 1. In order to satisfy a k, 1 ∗ a k, 3 = 0 , a k, 1 ∗ a k, 4 = 0 , a k, 3 ∗ a k, 4 = 0 , there must exist r ∈ { 1 , 2 } such that the entries A k, 3 ,r = A k, 4 ,r = 1. Since A k does not contain any all-ones column, there must also exist s ∈ [ p k ] ∩{ 1 , 3 , 4 } c such that the entry A k,s,r = 0. Since a k, 1 ∗ a k,s = 0 , a k, 3 ∗ a k,s = 0 , a k, 4 ∗ a k,s = 0 , we know the entries A k,s, 3 -r = A k,s, 3 = A k,s, 4 = 1. This shows that the row a ⊤ k,s has at least three non-zero entries, again raising a contradiction.

texts.orig: Proof of Lemma C.10. Without loss of generality, we let P k = I p k in (3.5), then we have entries A k,t,t = A k,p k -1 + t,t = 1 for all t ∈ [ p k -1 ]. Since A ∈ A 2 , 2 , the r th and s th columns of A k are distinct, implying that there exists a row w ∈ [ p k ] such that A k,w,r = A k,w,s . If A k,w,r = 1 and A k,w,s = 0, then we have a k,r ∗ a k,w = e r , so result (i) holds with i = r and j = w . If A k,w,r = 0 and A k,w,s = 1, then we have a k,r ∗ a k,w = e s , so result (ii) holds with i = r , j = p k -1 + r , and ℓ = w .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: D Posterior Consistency
texts.text: D Posterior Consistency

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we present the proofs of our posterior consistency theorems. We start by providing background on standard techniques in Section D.1, including Schwartz' theorem and Doob's theorem for (strong) posterior consistency. The Schwartz' theorem works with the Wasserstein space of probability measures. By establishing certain topological properties of the parameter space, this consistency of probability measures can be transformed into consistency of model parameters, leading to the proof of Theorem 3.5 in Section D.2. Proofs of auxiliary lemmas on these topological properties are provided in Section D.3. The Doob's theorem works directly with model parameters, but it requires identifiability of the model. It does not automatically apply here due to the non-trivial type of non-identifiability problem of our model, so we introduce the idea of quotient parameters and study the Borel σ -algebra of the quotient parameter space in Section D.4. Theorem 3.8 is then proven in Section D.5 by applying intermediate steps of the Doob's theorem to the quotient parameters.
texts.text: In this section, we present the proofs of our posterior consistency theorems. We start by providing background on standard techniques in Section D.1, including Schwartz' theorem and Doob's theorem for (strong) posterior consistency. The Schwartz' theorem works with the Wasserstein space of probability measures. By establishing certain topological properties of the parameter space, this consistency of probability measures can be transformed into consistency of model parameters, leading to the proof of Theorem 3.5 in Section D.2. Proofs of auxiliary lemmas on these topological properties are provided in Section D.3. The Doob's theorem works directly with model parameters, but it requires identifiability of the model. It does not automatically apply here due to the non-trivial type of non-identifiability problem of our model, so we introduce the idea of quotient parameters and study the Borel σ -algebra of the quotient parameter space in Section D.4. Theorem 3.8 is then proven in Section D.5 by applying intermediate steps of the Doob's theorem to the quotient parameters.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: D.1 Schwartz' Theorem and Doob's Theorem
texts.text: D.1 Schwartz' Theorem and Doob's Theorem

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Before we present proofs of posterior consistency for our model, we first provide an overview of the general posterior consistency theories, including Schwartz' Theorem and Doob's Theorem. We consider a general sample space ( X , X ) and a family of models with distributions P θ indexed by θ ∈ Θ with Θ the parameter space.
texts.text: Before we present proofs of posterior consistency for our model, we first provide an overview of the general posterior consistency theories, including Schwartz' Theorem and Doob's Theorem. We consider a general sample space ( X , X ) and a family of models with distributions P θ indexed by θ ∈ Θ with Θ the parameter space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Schwartz's theory of posterior consistency (Schwartz, 1965) directly works with the probability densities. Letting ν be a dominating measure on X , we view each distribution P as parameterized by its density p with respect to ν . The class of densities P is the parameter space, equipped with the weak topology and the corresponding Borel σ -algebra P . Let observations { X ( n ) } ∞ n =1 i.i.d. follow the true distribution with density p ∗ ∈ P , such that ( p ∗ ) ∞ denotes the joint distribution of X (1: ∞ ) . We impose the prior distribution Π on P and denote the posterior distribution given X (1: n ) by Π n .
texts.text: Schwartz's theory of posterior consistency (Schwartz, 1965) directly works with the probability densities. Letting ν be a dominating measure on X , we view each distribution P as parameterized by its density p with respect to ν . The class of densities P is the parameter space, equipped with the weak topology and the corresponding Borel σ -algebra P . Let observations { X ( n ) } ∞ n =1 i.i.d. follow the true distribution with density p ∗ ∈ P , such that ( p ∗ ) ∞ denotes the joint distribution of X (1: ∞ ) . We impose the prior distribution Π on P and denote the posterior distribution given X (1: n ) by Π n .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let d be a metric on P that induces the weak topology, e.g. the Wasserstein distance. An ϵ -neighborhood of p ∗ in P is naturally defined as { p ∈ P : d ( p, p ∗ ) < ϵ } . Using the KullbackLeibler divergence D KL ( q, p ) := ∫ X q log q p d ν , we also define an ϵ -KL neighborhood of p ∗ to be the set { p ∈ P : D KL ( p ∗ , p ) < ϵ } . We notice that by Pinsker's inequality, every open neighborhood of p ∗ contains an ϵ -KL neighborhood of p ∗ for some ϵ > 0. For the prior distribution Π, we say p ∗ belongs to the KL support of Π if any KL neighborhood of p ∗ has positive prior probability, i.e. for all ϵ > 0, Π { p ∈ P : D KL ( p ∗ , p ) < ϵ } > 0.
texts.text: Let d be a metric on P that induces the weak topology, e.g. the Wasserstein distance. An ϵ -neighborhood of p ∗ in P is naturally defined as { p ∈ P : d ( p, p ∗ ) < ϵ } . Using the KullbackLeibler divergence D KL ( q, p ) := ∫ X q log q p d ν , we also define an ϵ -KL neighborhood of p ∗ to be the set { p ∈ P : D KL ( p ∗ , p ) < ϵ } . We notice that by Pinsker's inequality, every open neighborhood of p ∗ contains an ϵ -KL neighborhood of p ∗ for some ϵ > 0. For the prior distribution Π, we say p ∗ belongs to the KL support of Π if any KL neighborhood of p ∗ has positive prior probability, i.e. for all ϵ > 0, Π { p ∈ P : D KL ( p ∗ , p ) < ϵ } > 0.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem D.1 (Schwartz' theorem) . If p ∗ is in the KL support of Π , then for any open neighborhood O of p ∗ , we have
texts.text: Theorem D.1 (Schwartz' theorem) . If p ∗ is in the KL support of Π , then for any open neighborhood O of p ∗ , we have

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem D.1 states that the posterior distribution Π n is (strongly) consistent for any true density p ∗ in the KL support of the prior distribution Π. A proof can be found in Ghosal and Van der Vaart (2017) Section 6.4. To apply Theorem D.1 to our model, we notice that a gap exists between the consistency of the probability density p and the consistency of our model parameters ( A , Θ ). These two notions of consistency can be bridged by utilizing the continuity of p in ( A , Θ ) and enforcing constraints on the topological properties of our parameter space R . This will be formally shown in the proof of Theorem 3.5.

texts.orig: Theorem D.2 is based on an application of Doob's martingale convergence theorem (Durrett, 2019), see Ghosal and Van der Vaart (2017) Section 6.2 for a proof. The measurability of θ with respect to the Q -completion of σ ( X (1: ∞ ) ) in Theorem D.2 can be guaranteed by the slightly stronger condition of θ = f ( X (1: ∞ ) ) P θ -a.s. for every θ ∈ Θ and some measurable function f : X ∞ → Θ. A sufficient condition is model identifiability, as indicated in the following proposition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proof of Proposition D.3 can also be found in Ghosal and Van der Vaart (2017) Section 6.2. We note that the Borel measurability of θ → P θ ( X ) and the parameter space Θ being the Borel subset of a Polish space is important, because the proof utilizes the Kuratowski's theorem (Srivastava, 2008) to transform measurability of P θ with respect to σ ( X (1: ∞ ) ) to measurability of θ with respect to σ ( X (1: ∞ ) ). Kuratowski's theorem states that the inverse of a one-to-one Borel map between Polish spaces is also Borel measurable, which does not hold in general for other measurable maps or general spaces.
texts.text: The proof of Proposition D.3 can also be found in Ghosal and Van der Vaart (2017) Section 6.2. Kuratowski's theorem states that the inverse of a one-to-one Borel map between Polish spaces is also Borel measurable, which does not hold in general for other measurable maps or general spaces.

texts.orig: For our model with some parameter space A × T , we notice that neither Theorem D.2 nor Proposition D.3 directly apply, due to the trivial type of non-identifiability in Perm A×T ( A , Θ ). Recall that in Definition 3.3, we have loosened the requirement of posterior consistency in our model from the posterior distribution asymptotically concentrating at ( A , Θ ) to asymptotically having support within Perm A×T ( A , Θ ). We eliminate the trivial type of non-identifiability by considering the quotient parameter space given by the equivalence relation of ( A , Θ ) ∼ ( A ′ , Θ ′ ) for all ( A ′ , Θ ′ ) ∈ Perm A×T ( A , Θ ). We will show properties of the quotient Borel-algebra and prove Theorem 3.8 using Proposition D.3 later this section.

We will show properties of the quotient Borel-algebra and prove Theorem 3.8 using Proposition D.3 later this section.

texts.orig: which belongs to the space of probability measures P := S |X K |-1 on X K , i.e. the probability simplex. Recalling that the space A 0 × T 0 is equipped with the L 1 distance, we let its subspace R⊂A 0 ×T 0 inherit the same metric. Since X K is discrete and finite-dimensional, the weak topology on P is the same as the topology induced by the L 1 distance inherited as a subspace of R |X K | . Our model specifications in (2.5) and (2.6) ensure for every X K ∈ X K that the probability P ( X K | A , Θ ) is a continuous function of A and Θ . We therefore find that the map L : R→P is continuous.

texts.orig: D.4 Quotient Parameter Space and Its Properties
texts.text: D.4 Quotient Parameter Space and Its Properties

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Section D.1, to apply Proposition D.3 and prove Doob's posterior consistency for our model, we need to get rid of the trivial non-identifiability issue coming from node permutations in each layer of our Bayesian network. We will consider the quotient of parameter space R defined by the equivalence relation of node permutations. We show some properties of the quotient parameter spaces in the following and prove Theorem 3.8 in the following subsection.
texts.text: As discussed in Section D.1, to apply Proposition D.3 and prove Doob's posterior consistency for our model, we need to get rid of the trivial non-identifiability issue coming from node permutations in each layer of our Bayesian network. We will consider the quotient of parameter space R defined by the equivalence relation of node permutations. We show some properties of the quotient parameter spaces in the following and prove Theorem 3.8 in the following subsection.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider a parameter space R ⊂ A 0 × T 0 that is invariant to node permutations, i.e. satisfying Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Perm A 0 ×T 0 ( A , Θ ) for all ( A , Θ ) ∈ R . Recall that R is endowed with the L 1 -distance and the induced metric topology. Let R denote its Borel σ -algebra. We notice that the binary relation of ( A , Θ ) , ( A ′ , Θ ′ ) ∈ R defined by
texts.text: We consider a parameter space R ⊂ A 0 × T 0 that is invariant to node permutations, i.e. satisfying Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Perm A 0 ×T 0 ( A , Θ ) for all ( A , Θ ) ∈ R . Recall that R is endowed with the L 1 -distance and the induced metric topology. Let R denote its Borel σ -algebra. We notice that the binary relation of ( A , Θ ) , ( A ′ , Θ ′ ) ∈ R defined by

texts.label: text
texts.orig: is an equivalence relation. For notational simplicity, we also use Perm R ( A , Θ ) to denote the equivalence class of ( A , Θ ) in R . Letting Q := { Perm R ( A , Θ ) : ( A , Θ ) ∈ R } denote the collection of all the equivalence classes, we can define the map φ : R → Q as φ ( A , Θ ) := Perm R ( A , Θ ). Since φ is surjective, it induces the quotient topology on Q such that a set U ⊂ Q is open if and only if φ -1 ( U ) is open in R . We denote the Borel σ -algebra of the quotient topology on Q by Q .
texts.text: is an equivalence relation. For notational simplicity, we also use Perm R ( A , Θ ) to denote the equivalence class of ( A , Θ ) in R . Letting Q := { Perm R ( A , Θ ) : ( A , Θ ) ∈ R } denote the collection of all the equivalence classes, we can define the map φ : R → Q as φ ( A , Θ ) := Perm R ( A , Θ ). Since φ is surjective, it induces the quotient topology on Q such that a set U ⊂ Q is open if and only if φ -1 ( U ) is open in R . We denote the Borel σ -algebra of the quotient topology on Q by Q .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By definition of quotient topology, we know that the quotient map φ is Borel measurable, i.e. for any B ∈ Q we have φ -1 ( B ) ∈ R . Furthermore, the following lemma shows that the quotient map φ also maps Borel sets in R to Borel sets in Q . Note that this is a property specific for our model, which does not hold for general quotient maps.
texts.text: By definition of quotient topology, we know that the quotient map φ is Borel measurable, i.e. for any B ∈ Q we have φ -1 ( B ) ∈ R . Furthermore, the following lemma shows that the quotient map φ also maps Borel sets in R to Borel sets in Q . Note that this is a property specific for our model, which does not hold for general quotient maps.

texts.orig: D.5 Proof of Theorem 3.8
texts.text: D.5 Proof of Theorem 3.8

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After introducing the quotient parameter space and its useful properties in Section D.4, we are now ready to prove Theorem 3.8 on Doob's posterior consistency of our model using Theorem D.2, Proposition D.3, and intermediate results from the proof of Theorem 3.7 in Section B.6.
texts.text: After introducing the quotient parameter space and its useful properties in Section D.4, we are now ready to prove Theorem 3.8 on Doob's posterior consistency of our model using Theorem D.2, Proposition D.3, and intermediate results from the proof of Theorem 3.7 in Section B.6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof of Theorem 3.8. In Section D.4, we have assumed the parameter space R ⊂ A 0 ×T 0 to be invariant to node permutations, i.e. Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Perm A 0 ×T 0 ( A , Θ ) for all ( A , Θ ) ∈ R . While this is not assumed in Theorem 3.8, we can extend the parameter space R to ∪ r ∈R Perm R ( r ) and put zero prior mass on the extended regions. Note that ∪ r ∈R Perm R ( r ) is a Borel subset of A 2 ×T 0 as long as R is. Therefore, without loss of generality, we will assume the parameter space R to be invariant to node permutations.
texts.text: Proof of Theorem 3.8. In Section D.4, we have assumed the parameter space R ⊂ A 0 ×T 0 to be invariant to node permutations, i.e. Perm R ( A , Θ ) = Perm A 0 ×T 0 ( A , Θ ) for all ( A , Θ ) ∈ R . While this is not assumed in Theorem 3.8, we can extend the parameter space R to ∪ r ∈R Perm R ( r ) and put zero prior mass on the extended regions. Note that ∪ r ∈R Perm R ( r ) is a Borel subset of A 2 ×T 0 as long as R is. Therefore, without loss of generality, we will assume the parameter space R to be invariant to node permutations.

Using the continuity of φ and Lemma D.7, this suggests that the quotient space φ ( A 2 ×T 0 ) is a complete separable metric space under the d Q metric and hence a Polish space.

texts.orig: E Posterior Computation: A Complete Guide
texts.text: E Posterior Computation: A Complete Guide

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we provide a complete guide of posterior computation for our model, including the details of our data augmented Gibbs sampler in Section E.1, its subsampling version in Section E.2, and the essentials of our spectral initialization algorithm in Section E.3. Fo ease of reading, we defer a literature overview of subsampling MCMC and related discussions to Section F, and also defer the preliminaries and technical details of our spectral initialization algorithm to Section G.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: E.2 Subsampling Gibbs Sampler
texts.text: E.2 Subsampling Gibbs Sampler

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As the sample size N and the dimension p get larger, the Gibbs sampler often experiences a high computational burden. The main computational bottlenecks come from sampling the latent adjacency matrices X (1: N ) and the connection matrices A , both of which have linear complexity on the sample size N . The sampling of X (1: N ) can be accelerated by drawing the latent X (1) , . . . , X ( N ) in parallel, due to their conditional independence. To boost the scalability in sampling the parameters A and Θ , it is worth considering whether there is a more efficient alternative way to evaluate their full conditional distributions. A commonly used approach is subsampling MCMC, in which we aim at reducing the complexity of likelihood evaluation by designing a computationally efficient surrogate likelihood function (Quiroz et al., 2018a,c). See Section F.1 for an overview of related literature. We next adapt this idea to our Gibbs sampler.
texts.text: As the sample size N and the dimension p get larger, the Gibbs sampler often experiences a high computational burden. The main computational bottlenecks come from sampling the latent adjacency matrices X (1: N ) and the connection matrices A , both of which have linear complexity on the sample size N . The sampling of X (1: N ) can be accelerated by drawing the latent X (1) , . . . , X ( N ) in parallel, due to their conditional independence. To boost the scalability in sampling the parameters A and Θ , it is worth considering whether there is a more efficient alternative way to evaluate their full conditional distributions. A commonly used approach is subsampling MCMC, in which we aim at reducing the complexity of likelihood evaluation by designing a computationally efficient surrogate likelihood function (Quiroz et al., 2018a,c). See Section F.1 for an overview of related literature. We next adapt this idea to our Gibbs sampler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After data augmentation, the latent variables of our model include X (1: N ) and ω (1: N ) , where ω ( n ) := { ω ( n ) k,i,j : k ∈ [ K ] , ( i, j ) ∈ ⟨ p k ⟩ } denotes the collection of augmented variables associated with sample n . At each iteration of our subsampling Gibbs sampler, we start by drawing X ( n ) , ω ( n ) in parallel across n from their standard full conditional distributions. We then uniformly sample without replacement a subset B of fixed cardinality |B| from the index set [ N ]. Using this subset of data and latent variables, we obtain an estimator for the data augmented log-likelihood
texts.text: After data augmentation, the latent variables of our model include X (1: N ) and ω (1: N ) , where ω ( n ) := { ω ( n ) k,i,j : k ∈ [ K ] , ( i, j ) ∈ ⟨ p k ⟩ } denotes the collection of augmented variables associated with sample n . At each iteration of our subsampling Gibbs sampler, we start by drawing X ( n ) , ω ( n ) in parallel across n from their standard full conditional distributions. We then uniformly sample without replacement a subset B of fixed cardinality |B| from the index set [ N ]. Using this subset of data and latent variables, we obtain an estimator for the data augmented log-likelihood

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ̂ ℓ ( A , Θ ) := N |B| ∑ n ∈B log P ( X ( n ) , ω ( n ) | A , Θ ) . (E.1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With this estimator ̂ ℓ ( A , Θ ), we could derive the approximate full conditional distributions of A , Θ and sample them in blocks in the same way as our standard Gibbs sampler.
texts.text: With this estimator ̂ ℓ ( A , Θ ), we could derive the approximate full conditional distributions of A , Θ and sample them in blocks in the same way as our standard Gibbs sampler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a type of approximate MCMC algorithm (Johndrow et al., 2015; Johndrow and Mattingly, 2017b), the stationary distribution of the subsampling Gibbs sampler is different from the standard Gibbs sampler. For the subset ratio of |B| N = 1%, we numerically verify this distance to be small through simulation study in Section H. Our posterior computation involves iterations of the subsampling Gibbs sampler followed by iterations of the standard Gibbs sampler. The subsampling Gibbs samples facilitate fast convergence to the posterior mode after initialization, while posterior inference is conducted based on the standard Gibbs samples. See Section F.2 for further discussions.
texts.text: As a type of approximate MCMC algorithm (Johndrow et al., 2015; Johndrow and Mattingly, 2017b), the stationary distribution of the subsampling Gibbs sampler is different from the standard Gibbs sampler. For the subset ratio of |B| N = 1%, we numerically verify this distance to be small through simulation study in Section H. Our posterior computation involves iterations of the subsampling Gibbs sampler followed by iterations of the standard Gibbs sampler. The subsampling Gibbs samples facilitate fast convergence to the posterior mode after initialization, while posterior inference is conducted based on the standard Gibbs samples. See Section F.2 for further discussions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: E.3 Spectral Initialization
texts.text: E.3 Spectral Initialization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now present an additional technique for accelerating posterior computation. The Degree Corrected Mixed Membership (DCMM) model (Jin et al., 2023) is proposed to detect communities in a single sparse network, allowing mixed membership and node degree heterogeneity. In the following, we establish a somewhat surprising link between the DCMM and our model. Using a multilayer hierarchical extension of their Mixed-Score algorithm, we can efficiently find a spectral initialization of our connection matrices A and significantly reduce the burn-in period needed in our MCMC sampler.
texts.text: We now present an additional technique for accelerating posterior computation. The Degree Corrected Mixed Membership (DCMM) model (Jin et al., 2023) is proposed to detect communities in a single sparse network, allowing mixed membership and node degree heterogeneity. In the following, we establish a somewhat surprising link between the DCMM and our model. Using a multilayer hierarchical extension of their Mixed-Score algorithm, we can efficiently find a spectral initialization of our connection matrices A and significantly reduce the burn-in period needed in our MCMC sampler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a single network with p nodes and q communities, DCMM models adjacency matrix Y as
texts.text: For a single network with p nodes and q communities, DCMM models adjacency matrix Y as

texts.orig: In our multilayer extension of Mixed-SCORE algorithm, we will recover the connection matrices A in a bottom-up fashion, starting from A K of the observed layer. By applying the Mixed-SCORE algorithm to 1 N X ( n ) K , we obtain an estimate of Π := diag( A K 1 ) -1 A K and truncate it into an estimate of A K through A K,i,j = 1 Π i,j ≥ S -1 . We then pick the p K -1 rows in Π closest to the p K -1 vertices of simplex S p K -1 -1 and select the adjacency sub-matrices ˜ X (1: N ) K -1 by keeping only these p k -1 rows and columns of X (1: N ) K . By repeatedly applying this approach to ˜ X (1: N ) K -1 , ˜ X (1: N ) K -2 , . . . , we sequentially obtain initial estimates of A K -1 , A K -2 , . . . , which together form the spectral initialization of our connection matrices A . We defer the details of this algorithm to Section G, along with a thorough overview of the DCMM and the proof of Theorem E.1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we provide a brief overview of subsampling MCMC methods in Section F.1 and discuss some further variants of our subsampling Gibbs sampler in Section F.2.
texts.text: In this section, we provide a brief overview of subsampling MCMC methods in Section F.1 and discuss some further variants of our subsampling Gibbs sampler in Section F.2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: F.1 Brief Overview of Subsampling MCMC
texts.text: F.1 Brief Overview of Subsampling MCMC

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many different versions of subsampling MCMC algorithms have been proposed in the past decade. As a crucial step in any MCMC algorithm, the evaluation of model likelihood often has very high computational complexity when the sample size of data is large. To speed up the MCMC algorithm, we could approximate the model log-likelihood with an estimator based on only a subset of data. For MCMC algorithms based on stochastic differential equations, the approximated versions include stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (Welling and Teh, 2011) and other variants (Ahn et al., 2012; Ma et al., 2015; Baker et al., 2019). For Metropolis-Hastings algorithms that involve likelihoods in computation of acceptance ratios, efficient estimators for the acceptance ratio based on a subset of data are available (Korattikara et al., 2014; Bardenet et al., 2014, 2017). To reduce the variance of the log-likelihood estimator, different control variate methods (Quiroz et al., 2018a,b) and subsample weighting methods (Maire et al., 2019) have also been proposed.
texts.text: Many different versions of subsampling MCMC algorithms have been proposed in the past decade. As a crucial step in any MCMC algorithm, the evaluation of model likelihood often has very high computational complexity when the sample size of data is large. To speed up the MCMC algorithm, we could approximate the model log-likelihood with an estimator based on only a subset of data. For MCMC algorithms based on stochastic differential equations, the approximated versions include stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (Welling and Teh, 2011) and other variants (Ahn et al., 2012; Ma et al., 2015; Baker et al., 2019). For Metropolis-Hastings algorithms that involve likelihoods in computation of acceptance ratios, efficient estimators for the acceptance ratio based on a subset of data are available (Korattikara et al., 2014; Bardenet et al., 2014, 2017). To reduce the variance of the log-likelihood estimator, different control variate methods (Quiroz et al., 2018a,b) and subsample weighting methods (Maire et al., 2019) have also been proposed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the numerous subsampling MCMC algorithm, it is important to note that all of them fall into the category of approximate MCMC methods, which do not exactly sample from the target distribution. Theoretical tools have been developed to bound the distance between the stationary distribution of approximate MCMC method and the target distribution when the original MCMC method is uniformly ergodic (Johndrow et al., 2015; Johndrow and Mattingly, 2017a) or geometrically ergodic (Pillai and Smith, 2014; Johndrow and Mattingly, 2017b; Rudolf and Schweizer, 2018; Negrea and Rosenthal, 2021).
texts.text: Despite the numerous subsampling MCMC algorithm, it is important to note that all of them fall into the category of approximate MCMC methods, which do not exactly sample from the target distribution. Theoretical tools have been developed to bound the distance between the stationary distribution of approximate MCMC method and the target distribution when the original MCMC method is uniformly ergodic (Johndrow et al., 2015; Johndrow and Mattingly, 2017a) or geometrically ergodic (Pillai and Smith, 2014; Johndrow and Mattingly, 2017b; Rudolf and Schweizer, 2018; Negrea and Rosenthal, 2021).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: F.2 Discussions of Subsampling Gibbs Sampler
texts.text: F.2 Discussions of Subsampling Gibbs Sampler

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now discuss a few possible variants of our subsampling Gibbs sampler.
texts.text: We now discuss a few possible variants of our subsampling Gibbs sampler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the current subsampling Gibbs sampler, while a subset of samples B is randomly selected at the start of each iteration, all the augmented variables ω ( n ) k,i,j 's are drawn from their full conditional distributions. These augmented variables can be sampled in parallel efficiently, but the computational complexity will be high when parallel computing is not available. As a compromise, we could instead draw only the subset of augmented variables ω ( n ) k,i,j with n ∈ B . While the resulting Markov chain has a stationary distribution that is theoretically further from the desired posterior distribution, the difference is observed to be small in practice.
texts.text: In the current subsampling Gibbs sampler, while a subset of samples B is randomly selected at the start of each iteration, all the augmented variables ω ( n ) k,i,j 's are drawn from their full conditional distributions. These augmented variables can be sampled in parallel efficiently, but the computational complexity will be high when parallel computing is not available. As a compromise, we could instead draw only the subset of augmented variables ω ( n ) k,i,j with n ∈ B . While the resulting Markov chain has a stationary distribution that is theoretically further from the desired posterior distribution, the difference is observed to be small in practice.

texts.orig: Other surrogate functions of the log-likelihood could potentially also be designed, in order to further reduce the evaluation time or improve the approximation quality. In current literature (Quiroz et al., 2018c), there are ideas of designing surrogate functions based on Taylor expansions or clustering of the data. We leave this for future work.

In current literature (Quiroz et al., 2018c), there are ideas of designing surrogate functions based on Taylor expansions or clustering of the data. We leave this for future work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: G.1 Preliminaries of DCMM and Mixed-SCORE Algorithm
texts.text: G.1 Preliminaries of DCMM and Mixed-SCORE Algorithm

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Degree Corrected Mixed Membership (DCMM) model (Jin et al., 2023) is designed to detect communities in a single sparse network, allowing mixed membership and node degree heterogeneity. An elementary introduction to the DCMM model and the Mixed-SCORE algorithm is provided here. We refer readers to Jin et al. (2023) for a complete study.
texts.text: The Degree Corrected Mixed Membership (DCMM) model (Jin et al., 2023) is designed to detect communities in a single sparse network, allowing mixed membership and node degree heterogeneity. An elementary introduction to the DCMM model and the Mixed-SCORE algorithm is provided here. We refer readers to Jin et al. (2023) for a complete study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a single sparse network with a p × p adjacency matrix Y and ˜ p communities, the DCMM model assumes that the upper-triangular entries of Y are conditionally independent given Bernoulli parameters:
texts.text: For a single sparse network with a p × p adjacency matrix Y and ˜ p communities, the DCMM model assumes that the upper-triangular entries of Y are conditionally independent given Bernoulli parameters:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P ( Y i,j = 1 | D , Π , Z ) = D i D j π ⊤ i Z π j ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where D i > 0 denotes the degree of node i , π i ∈ R ˜ p × 1 denotes the mixed membership vector of node i , and Z ∈ R ˜ p × ˜ p is a symmetric matrix with positive entries. The diagonal matrix D = diag( D 1 , . . . , D p ), the p × ˜ p membership matrix Π with its i th row being π i , and the matrix Z together compose the parameters of the DCMM model. Intuitively, the entry Z i,j ( i, j ∈ [1 , ˜ p ]) of Z models the probability of connection between a node purely belonging to community i and a node purely belonging to community j , as in stochastic block models (Holland et al., 1983). Each row vector π i in the mixed membership matrix Π belongs to the probability simplex, with all entries non-negative and summing up to one. Therefore, the probability of connection between two nodes is a weighted average of Z i,j 's over all pairs of communities ( i, j )'s that the two nodes partially belong to. Different nodes may have different propensity of being connected to other nodes regardless of their community memberships, which is known as the degree heterogeneity issue. The degree correction matrix D is introduced to adjust for such degree heterogeneity. In matrix form, we have
texts.text: where D i > 0 denotes the degree of node i , π i ∈ R ˜ p × 1 denotes the mixed membership vector of node i , and Z ∈ R ˜ p × ˜ p is a symmetric matrix with positive entries. texts.text: E [ Y | D , Π , Z ] = DΠZΠ ⊤ D .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given the observation Y , the Mixed-SCORE algorithm estimates Π in three steps: spectral clustering on ratios-of-eigenvectors (SCORE), vertex hunting, and membership reconstruction.
texts.text: Given the observation Y , the Mixed-SCORE algorithm estimates Π in three steps: spectral clustering on ratios-of-eigenvectors (SCORE), vertex hunting, and membership reconstruction.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. In the SCORE step, for the top ˜ p eigenvectors ξ 1 , . . . , ξ ˜ p of Y , we divide ξ 2 , . . . , ξ ˜ p entrywise over ξ 1 and stack the resulted ˜ p -1 vectors by columns into a matrix R ∈ R p × ˜ p -1 . For intuition purposes, we also consider the matrix ˜ R obtained by applying this SCORE step to E [ Y | D , Π , Z ] instead of Y , since the difference between R and ˜ R is proven to be small. It is also shown that the rows of ˜ R form a simplex in R ˜ p -1 . When each community possesses at least one pure node (a node that belongs purely to this community), the vertices of this simplex corresponds to the rows in ˜ R of the pure nodes.
texts.text: In the SCORE step, for the top ˜ p eigenvectors ξ 1 , . . . , ξ ˜ p of Y , we divide ξ 2 , . . . , ξ ˜ p entrywise over ξ 1 and stack the resulted ˜ p -1 vectors by columns into a matrix R ∈ R p × ˜ p -1 . For intuition purposes, we also consider the matrix ˜ R obtained by applying this SCORE step to E [ Y | D , Π , Z ] instead of Y , since the difference between R and ˜ R is proven to be small. It is also shown that the rows of ˜ R form a simplex in R ˜ p -1 . When each community possesses at least one pure node (a node that belongs purely to this community), the vertices of this simplex corresponds to the rows in ˜ R of the pure nodes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. In the vertex hunting step, we identify the vertices of this simplex using existing methods such as successive projection (Ara´ ujo et al., 2001; Nascimento and Dias, 2005). This allows us to find out the pure nodes of the model. To improve the robustness of this step, it is often helpful to conduct k -means clustering of the rows in R before finding the vertices, know as sketched vertex search (SVS, Jin et al., 2023).
texts.text: In the vertex hunting step, we identify the vertices of this simplex using existing methods such as successive projection (Ara´ ujo et al., 2001; Nascimento and Dias, 2005). This allows us to find out the pure nodes of the model. To improve the robustness of this step, it is often helpful to conduct k -means clustering of the rows in R before finding the vertices, know as sketched vertex search (SVS, Jin et al., 2023).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. In the membership reconstruction step, given the pure nodes, the mixed memberships of each node can be recovered through solving linear equation systems.
texts.text: In the membership reconstruction step, given the pure nodes, the mixed memberships of each node can be recovered through solving linear equation systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mixed memberships Π can be exactly recovered when the Mixed-SCORE algorithm is applied to E [ Y | D , Π , Z ]. In practice, only Y is known and the Mixed-SCORE algorithm is applied to Y , but the estimator of Π obtained remains valid and consistent since the difference ∥ Y -E [ Y | D , Π , Z ] ∥ 2 can be bounded to be small.
texts.text: The mixed memberships Π can be exactly recovered when the Mixed-SCORE algorithm is applied to E [ Y | D , Π , Z ]. In practice, only Y is known and the Mixed-SCORE algorithm is applied to Y , but the estimator of Π obtained remains valid and consistent since the difference ∥ Y -E [ Y | D , Π , Z ] ∥ 2 can be bounded to be small.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: G.2 Our Spectral Initialization Algorithm
texts.text: G.2 Our Spectral Initialization Algorithm

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem E.1 shows that the sample average of adjacency matrices 1 N ∑ N n =1 X ( n ) 1 is close to the expected adjacency matrix DΠZΠ ⊤ D in a DCMM model, with the mixed membership matrix being Π = diag( A 1 1 ) -1 A 1 . Given Π , we can recover the connection matrix A 1 through the simple truncation A 1 ,i,j = 1 Π i,j ≥ S -1 . This suggests an algorithm for warmly initializing A for K = 1.
texts.text: Theorem E.1 shows that the sample average of adjacency matrices 1 N ∑ N n =1 X ( n ) 1 is close to the expected adjacency matrix DΠZΠ ⊤ D in a DCMM model, with the mixed membership matrix being Π = diag( A 1 1 ) -1 A 1 . Given Π , we can recover the connection matrix A 1 through the simple truncation A 1 ,i,j = 1 Π i,j ≥ S -1 . This suggests an algorithm for warmly initializing A for K = 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When our Bayesian network has more than two layers (i.e. K > 1), we can take the following approach to iteratively apply the above algorithm for K = 1 and initialize the connection matrices A k for k = K,K -1 , . . . , 1 in a bottom-up fashion. For each k ∈ [ K ], after initializing the connection matrix A k , we can compute the corresponding DCMM mixed membership matrix Π k = diag( A k 1 ) -1 A k . We pick the submatrix of Π k that is closest to an identity matrix, such that these selected p k -1 rows in Π k roughly corresponds to p k -1 distinct pure nodes. We keep only these p k -1 rows and columns of the adjacency matrices X (1: N ) k , leading to the p k -1 × p k -1 adjacency sub-matrices ˜ X (1: N ) k . The obtained adjacency sub-matrices will closely characterize the adjacency between the nodes in the ( k -1)th layer in the Bayesian network, to which the Mixed-SCORE algorithm and truncation can be applied again to initialize A k -1 . Repeated application of this approach for k = K,K -1 , . . . , 1 allows us to initialize all connection matrices A . We refer to this warm initialization algorithm as the Multilayer Mixed-SCORE algorithm and formalize it in Algorithm 1.

texts.text: H Simulation Study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we numerically evaluate our model consistency and posterior computation approaches through a simulated example. We present the main results in Section H.1 and additional results with discussions in Section H.2.
texts.text: In this section, we numerically evaluate our model consistency and posterior computation approaches through a simulated example. We present the main results in Section H.1 and additional results with discussions in Section H.2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To prepare for the application to brain connectomics, we design our simulation to mimic the sample size and the dimension of the brain connectome data. Let there be N = 1000 samples of 68 × 68 observed adjacency matrix X (1: N ) K , generated from our model with the Bayesian network structure of ( K + 1) = 3 layers and p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 16 , p 2 = 68 nodes. In the data generating distribution, the continuous parameters of our model have true values specified as ν = 2 -p 0 ( p 0 -1) / 2 1 , C k = -7, and Γ k = 4 11 ⊤ +6 I , for all k ∈ [ K ]. The true value of each connection matrix A k takes the form ( I p k -1 M k B k ) ⊤ for submatrices M k ∈ M p k -1 and B k ∈ R p k -1 × ( p k -2 p k -1 ) (the exact value is given in Section H.2), with each row a ⊤ k,i of A k containing one or two entries of '1'. In this way, we have

texts.orig: We impose a prior distribution P ( A , Θ ) in the factorized form (4.1), with each independent block of parameters following a weakly informative prior. We let the prior of each a k,i be uniform over its space V k and the prior of ν be uniform over the probability simplex S p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 + . For the continuous parameters C and Γ , we follow Gelman (2006) and adopt the weakly informative prior proportional to the standard normal distribution N (0 , I ), such that each C k ∼ N (0 , 1) and each entry Γ k,i,j ∼ N + (0 , 1).
texts.text: We impose a prior distribution P ( A , Θ ) in the factorized form (4.1), with each independent block of parameters following a weakly informative prior. We let the prior of each a k,i be uniform over its space V k and the prior of ν be uniform over the probability simplex S p 0 ( p 0 -1) 2 -1 + . For the continuous parameters C and Γ , we follow Gelman (2006) and adopt the weakly informative prior proportional to the standard normal distribution N (0 , I ), such that each C k ∼ N (0 , 1) and each entry Γ k,i,j ∼ N + (0 , 1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We treat the number of nodes p k in each layer of the Bayesian network and the model parameters ( A , Θ ) as unknown. To determine p k , we conduct model selection based on the Watanabe-Akaike information criterion (WAIC) in Watanabe and Opper (2010). Given fixed choices of p k , we can then infer model parameters ( A , Θ ) using the posterior computation approaches in Section 4. Specifically, we consider all choices of p k satisfying p k ≥ 2 p k -1 for each k ∈ [ K ], which is necessary for both the strict and generic notions of identifiability. We further focus on p 0 ∈ [2 , 6] for better interpretability of the Bayesian network and also to prevent the parameter ν from being too highdimensional. For every such choice of p k , we use the spectral initialization approach in Section E.3 to initialize connection matrices A , then run the subsampling Gibbs sampler in Section E.2 with a 1% subset ratio for 10,000 iterations, and follow by running the standard Gibbs sampler in Section E.2 for 100 iterations. The 10,000 iterations of the subsampling Gibbs sampler is used for quick convergence from the spectral initialization, whereas the 100 iterations of the standard Gibbs sampler are used for posterior inference. We use the Gelman-Rubin statistic (Gelman and Rubin, 1992) and the Geweke statistic (Geweke, 1992) for convergence diagnosis of both the subsampling and the standard Gibbs samplers. Detailed results reported in Section H.2 provide evidence that the Markov chains of Gibbs samplers under each choice of p k have converged.
texts.text: We treat the number of nodes p k in each layer of the Bayesian network and the model parameters ( A , Θ ) as unknown. To determine p k , we conduct model selection based on the Watanabe-Akaike information criterion (WAIC) in Watanabe and Opper (2010). Given fixed choices of p k , we can then infer model parameters ( A , Θ ) using the posterior computation approaches in Section 4. Specifically, we consider all choices of p k satisfying p k ≥ 2 p k -1 for each k ∈ [ K ], which is necessary for both the strict and generic notions of identifiability. We further focus on p 0 ∈ [2 , 6] for better interpretability of the Bayesian network and also to prevent the parameter ν from being too highdimensional. For every such choice of p k , we use the spectral initialization approach in Section E.3 to initialize connection matrices A , then run the subsampling Gibbs sampler in Section E.2 with a 1% subset ratio for 10,000 iterations, and follow by running the standard Gibbs sampler in Section E.2 for 100 iterations. The 10,000 iterations of the subsampling Gibbs sampler is used for quick convergence from the spectral initialization, whereas the 100 iterations of the standard Gibbs sampler are used for posterior inference. We use the Gelman-Rubin statistic (Gelman and Rubin, 1992) and the Geweke statistic (Geweke, 1992) for convergence diagnosis of both the subsampling and the standard Gibbs samplers. Detailed results reported in Section H.2 provide evidence that the Markov chains of Gibbs samplers under each choice of p k have converged.

texts.orig: are drawn randomly from their weakly informative prior distributions and hence distant from their true values. In the 10,000 iterations of the subsampling Gibbs sampler, the parameters quickly converge towards their stationary distributions within the first 2,000 iterations and fluctuate for the next 8,000 iterations, exhibiting good mixing properties. In the following 100 Gibbs sampler iterations, the parameters further converge to their new stationary distributions, which are the desired posterior distributions of parameters. Despite the small subset ratio of |B| N = 1% in the subsampling Gibbs sampler, it has stationary distributions very close to the standard Gibbs sampler. Further comparison of stationary distributions in W 1 distance is provided in Section H.2. We also observe that the true values of parameters are recovered in the 100 iterations of the standard Gibbs sampler and uncertainty quantification can be conducted through Monte Carlo inference.

Despite the small subset ratio of |B| N = 1% in the subsampling Gibbs sampler, it has stationary distributions very close to the standard Gibbs sampler. Further comparison of stationary distributions in W 1 distance is provided in Section H.2. We also observe that the true values of parameters are recovered in the 100 iterations of the standard Gibbs sampler and uncertainty quantification can be conducted through Monte Carlo inference.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All the simulation results have justified (a) our posterior computation framework that combines spectral initialization, subsampling Gibbs sampler, and standard Gibbs sampler; and also (b) our model selection approach based on the WAIC criterion. We note that based on our preliminary simulations, the presented results from one trial are typical and similar to observations from repeated experiments and different data generating distributions. Therefore, we will be adopting the same computational framework in the following real data section when analyzing the brain connectome data, where no true values of model structure or model parameters are known.

Fixing K = 2, for each valid choice of p 0 and p 1 , we start with spectral initialization, run 10,000 iterations of subsampling Gibbs sampler with 1% subset ratio, and follow by 100 iterations of standard Gibbs sampler. texts.orig: We diagnose the convergence and mixing of the Gibbs samplers using Gelman-Rubin statistic (Gelman and Rubin, 1992) and Geweke statistic (Geweke, 1992). For every choice of p 0 , p 1 , we repeat the sampling process multiple times and obtain several Markov chains of samples from subsampling and standard Gibbs samplers for each dimension of the parameters ( A , Θ ). We take averages of the computed Gelman-Rubin and Geweke statistics over all dimensions of ( A , Θ ) and all choices of p 0 , p 1 , as reported in Table 10. All Gelman-Rubin statistics are below 1.1 and all Geweke statistics lie in the tails of the distribution N (0 , 1), which are good indicators for the convergence of our MCMC chains.

texts.orig: For the continuous parameters Γ k at each layer k , we plot their true values and their posterior means under both Gibbs samplers in Figure 12. Both subsampling Gibbs sampler and standard Gibbs sampler produce samples that are close to the true values of Γ 1 , Γ 2 . While the subsampling Gibbs sampler tends to underestimate some parameters in the first layer and overestimate some parameters in the second layer, the samples from the standard Gibbs sampler have demonstrated to estimate the true values of Γ consistently. The posterior estimates of the continuous parameters C 1 , C 2 are also found to demonstrate similar behaviors. This agrees with the trace plots of C k , Γ k shown in Figure 7 of the main paper.

texts.orig: As discussed in Section E.2 of the main paper, the stationary distribution of standard Gibbs sampler is the true posterior distribution, whereas the stationary distribution of subsampling Gibbs sampler only approximates the true posterior distribution. We now numerically compare the error of this approximation in W 1 -distance and justify the use of our subsampling Gibbs sampler. For any two probability measures µ, ν on a Polish space Z , the Wasserstein 1-distance between them is defined as W 1 ( µ, ν ) := inf π ∈ Γ( µ,ν ) ∫ Z×Z ∥ x -y ∥ 1 d π ( x, y ), where Γ( µ, ν ) denotes the collection of all couplings of µ, ν . For each entry of Γ 1 , Γ 2 , we compute the W 1 -distance between the empirical distributions of posterior samples under subsampling and standard Gibbs samplers. We present the ratio of W 1 -distance over the true value for each entry in Figure 13. We find for each entry of Γ 1 , Γ 2 that the W 1 approximation error of the stationary distribution for subsampling Gibbs sampler is relatively small compared to the scale of the true value. This is also consistent with our observations from Figure 7 of the main paper.
texts.text: As discussed in Section E.2 of the main paper, the stationary distribution of standard Gibbs sampler is the true posterior distribution, whereas the stationary distribution of subsampling Gibbs sampler only approximates the true posterior distribution. We now numerically compare the error of this approximation in W 1 -distance and justify the use of our subsampling Gibbs sampler. For any two probability measures µ, ν on a Polish space Z , the Wasserstein 1-distance between them is defined as W 1 ( µ, ν ) := inf π ∈ Γ( µ,ν ) ∫ Z×Z ∥ x -y ∥ 1 d π ( x, y ), where Γ( µ, ν ) denotes the collection of all couplings of µ, ν . For each entry of Γ 1 , Γ 2 , we compute the W 1 -distance between the empirical distributions of posterior samples under subsampling and standard Gibbs samplers. We present the ratio of W 1 -distance over the true value for each entry in Figure 13. We find for each entry of Γ 1 , Γ 2 that the W 1 approximation error of the stationary distribution for subsampling Gibbs sampler is relatively small compared to the scale of the true value. This is also consistent with our observations from Figure 7 of the main paper.

We provide the details of our model structure selection in Section I.1, deferred figures of Section 5.1 in Section I.2, deferred figures of Section 5.2 in Section I.3. the deferred figures of Section 5 and some additional discussions. As promised in Section 5, we also provide an additional data analysis of our model in Section I.4, where we relate the inferred latent features to cognitive traits and compare to baseline models such as DCMM (Jin et al., 2023).

texts.orig: We visualize the computed WAIC for each choice of p 0 , p 1 under sparsity hyperparameter S = 1 , 2 in Figure 14. We find that the landscape of WAIC over the p 0 -p 1 plane is approximately unimodal and smooth under each sparsity hyperparameter S . For S = 1, WAIC is minimized at p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21. For S = 2, WAIC is minimized at p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18. As expected, the optimal choice of p k is smaller under sparsity parameter S = 2 compared to S = 1, indicating that allowing overlapping memberships under S = 2 relieves the need of higher latent dimensions compared to S = 1. The WAIC for the optimal choice of p k is also much lower under S = 2 compared to S = 1.

texts.orig: I.2 Multi-resolution Partitioning and Clustering of Brain Regions
texts.text: I.2 Multi-resolution Partitioning and Clustering of Brain Regions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 5.1, we have visualized the marginal posterior distributions of the p 0 higher-level clusters and the p 1 lower-level clusters for the optimal model choice p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18 under sparsity hyperparameter S = 2. We now visualize for the optimal model choice p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21 under sparsity hyperparameter S = 1 in Figures 15(a) and 15(b). Observe that almost all lower-level and higher-level clusters are formed by brain regions that are geographically close together on the cerebral cortex even though we do not include spatial locations of nodes in our data analysis.
texts.text: In Section 5.1, we have visualized the marginal posterior distributions of the p 0 higher-level clusters and the p 1 lower-level clusters for the optimal model choice p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18 under sparsity hyperparameter S = 2. We now visualize for the optimal model choice p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21 under sparsity hyperparameter S = 1 in Figures 15(a) and 15(b). Observe that almost all lower-level and higher-level clusters are formed by brain regions that are geographically close together on the cerebral cortex even though we do not include spatial locations of nodes in our data analysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The learned clusters are highly interpretable. The lower-level (shallower-layer) clusters in Figure 15(b) form a partition of the cerebral cortex into 21 regions, which roughly correspond to the frontal lobes, parietal lobes, temporal lobes, occipital lobes of the left and right hemispheres of the brain, as well as some smaller regions. In the four higher-level (deeper-layer) clusters in Figure 15(a), two clusters roughly correspond to the left hemisphere, and two roughly correspond to the right hemisphere, nicely revealing the anatomical structure of human brains.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I.4 Relating Inferred Latent Features to Cognitive Traits
texts.text: I.4 Relating Inferred Latent Features to Cognitive Traits

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We next relate individual's cognitive traits to their brain connectivity. We use 33 categories of cognitive traits, including language skills measured by oral reading recognition, dexterity measured by the game of 9-hole pegboard, strength measured by grip strength dynamometry, dependence on tobacco, etc. Within each category, multiple ordinal or continuous measures are available, together forming 175 cognitive traits. To simplify the analysis, we focus on relating brain connectivity to the leading principal components (PCs) of the traits within each category, with these 33 PCs marginally transformed to Gaussianity in a first stage.
texts.text: We next relate individual's cognitive traits to their brain connectivity. We use 33 categories of cognitive traits, including language skills measured by oral reading recognition, dexterity measured by the game of 9-hole pegboard, strength measured by grip strength dynamometry, dependence on tobacco, etc. Within each category, multiple ordinal or continuous measures are available, together forming 175 cognitive traits. To simplify the analysis, we focus on relating brain connectivity to the leading principal components (PCs) of the traits within each category, with these 33 PCs marginally transformed to Gaussianity in a first stage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Instead of directly relating the very high-dimensional observed adjacency matrices X (1: N ) 2 to the cognitive traits, we focus on the latent adjacency matrices in the shallower latent layer of our model, i.e. X (1: N ) 1 . As explained in Section 2, the nodes in layer 1 can be viewed as the communities for the observed nodes in layer 2 and the adjacency matrix X ( n ) 1 represents the adjacency between these communities for each individual n . So intuitively, X ( n ) 1 should preserve the important adjacency information in X ( n ) 2 . We want to study if X (1: N ) 1 , which is learned in an unsupervised manner, is related to the PCs of cognitive traits.
texts.text: Instead of directly relating the very high-dimensional observed adjacency matrices X (1: N ) 2 to the cognitive traits, we focus on the latent adjacency matrices in the shallower latent layer of our model, i.e. X (1: N ) 1 . As explained in Section 2, the nodes in layer 1 can be viewed as the communities for the observed nodes in layer 2 and the adjacency matrix X ( n ) 1 represents the adjacency between these communities for each individual n . So intuitively, X ( n ) 1 should preserve the important adjacency information in X ( n ) 2 . We want to study if X (1: N ) 1 , which is learned in an unsupervised manner, is related to the PCs of cognitive traits.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MCMC samples of latent adjacency matrix X ( n ) 1 are not comparable when the corresponding samples of connection matrix A are different. In the remaining part of this section, for interpretability, we therefore condition on A being equal to its posterior mode A ∗ . For brain connectome data, the posterior mean of X ( n ) 1 often has many entries ≈ 0 or 1 for all individuals. We exclude these entries and focus on the active entries ( i, j ) having 1 N ∑ N n =1 E [ X ( n ) 1 ,i,j | X ( n ) 2 , A ∗ ] ∈ (0 . 01 , 0 . 99). For our best models selected in Section I.1 with p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 21 , p 2 = 68 for S = 1 and p 0 = 4 , p 1 = 18 , p 2 = 68 for S = 2, there remain around 100 active entries, forming the latent features of our model. This is a considerable reduction from the 2278 entries in an observed adjacency matrix X ( n ) 2 .

texts.orig: Since our spectral initialization is based on the Degree Corrected Mixed Membership model (DCMM, Jin et al., 2023) and its Mixed-SCORE algorithm, we compare our results with those obtained by applying DCMM to each individual network. This comparison will allow us to see the improvement in our model over the approximation given by simpler models. The DCMM is a state-of-the-art flexible method for single network community detection. Descriptions of DCMM are provided in Section E.3 and Section I. From (E.2), the entries in the latent probability matrix Z ( n ) for individual n are viewed as the latent features of DCMM and can be estimated using the Mixed-SCORE algorithm. The number of communities q in DCMM roughly corresponds to the number of nodes p 1 in the shallower latent layer of our Bayesian network. So for comparison with our model, we consider two DCMM models with q = 18 and 21, corresponding to the selected p 1 values under S = 2 and S = 1, respectively.
